clinical_signs: Partial-thickness burn ≥15% TBSA in adults or ≥10% TBSA in children
Special regions affected (hands, face, feet, perineum)
Burn in the very young or elderly
immediate_actions: Protect airway (consider laryngeal oedema with or without inhalation injury)
Cool burn if acute
Fluid resuscitation (Give fluid according to Parkland formula, insert urinary catheter)
Consider escharotomy for circumferential burns
Give tetanus toxoid
Manage acute pain
Place a nasogastric tube for feeding and give medication for gastric acid suppression
Admit to hospital

equipment_needed: sterile techniques for cleaning and debridement
non-adherent dressing (paraffin gauze or honey and ghee)
step_by_step_guide: Use sterile techniques for cleaning and debridement.
Remove loose, necrotic skin and broken, tense, or infected blisters.
Apply a non-adherent dressing and provide a moist healing environment.
Change the dressing daily.
Mobilize any burned joints twice a day and at dressing changes (move through range of motion, medicate for pain as needed).
If a burn encircles a limb, elevate any burned extremity and monitor it frequently.
Consider escharotomy for limb cyanosis, decreased pulses, or worsening neurological status.
Consider escharotomy in the severe burn patient with difficulty ventilating secondary to burned skin that limits chest movement.
Hold the eyes open (the patient may need a local anaesthetic to prevent blepharospasm).
Wash any chemicals out with cool, clean water for 15–20 minutes. Take care that run-off does not enter the other eye.
In the case of acids or alkalis, check the pH of the conjunctival fluid and continue irrigation until the pH is 7.4.
Do not let the patient rub the eyes.
Treat pain.
If light causes pain, cover the eye with a sterile pad.
Examine the eye.

clinical_signs: Muscarinic effects: DUMBBELS (defecation, urination, miosis, bronchospasm, bronchorrhea, emesis, lacrimation, salivation)
Nicotinic effects: weakness, fasciculation, paralysis, mydriasis

medical_records.adult_dosing: IM/IV: Give bolus 1–3 mg (aim for clear lungs, stable blood pressure and dry mucous membranes) THEN double initial dose if no improvement at 5 minutes. Once stable, initiate an infusion of atropine (20% of the total dose required to atropinize) as an hourly infusion.

IV: Initially 150 mg/kg over 15 minutes; then 50 mg/kg over 4 hours; then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours. Oral: Loading dose of 140 mg/kg; THEN 4 hours after loading dose, initiate maintenance dose of 70 mg/kg administered at 4-hour intervals for 17 doses.
side_effects_or_warnings: Anaphylactoid or hypersensitivity-like reactions including angioedema, bronchospasm/respiratory distress, hypotension, tachycardia or hypertension

equipment_needed: support material – plaster, pre-formed splints, modified local materials
bucket or pail of wet plaster
step_by_step_guide: Always explain to the patient what you are doing and why.
Treat pain prior to applying a splint.
Remove clothing to adequately visualize the injured extremity.
Check and document neurovascular status (circulation, motor, sensory) before and after application of the splint.
Cover open fractures or joints with saline moistened sterile gauze.
If the injured extremity is visibly deformed, first straighten (reduce) prior to the application of the splint.
Place the joint in the desired position prior to splinting.
If available, place a stockinette over the skin, extending 10–15 cm beyond the area to be splinted.
Wrap padding around the entire area to be splinted (at least 2–3 layers thick).
Measure the length of material needed to secure the limb.
Soak the plaster roll in a pail containing water at room temperature until the air bubbles cease to rise.
Smooth the wet plaster with the palm into a homogeneous layer.
Place the plaster splint over the area to be immobilized.
Fold the padding and stockinette back to secure the splint in place and form smooth rounded edges.
While still wet, mould the plaster to the limb contours and secure with an elastic bandage or gauze wrap.
side_effects_or_warnings: plaster becomes hot when wet and can cause skin burns
pressure sores from improper application or using fingertips to hold wet plaster
compartment syndrome from tight casts or dressings

medical_records.primary_topic: Patient instructions for splints and casts
home_care_instructions: Keep the splint dry at all times.
Do not try to scratch your skin under the cast or splint with any object, sharp or blunt.
For acute injuries, elevate the injured part for 24–48 hours and wiggle your fingers or toes frequently.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: your splint gets wet or becomes soft or broken
you experience numbness or tingling, or have difficulty moving your fingers or toes
you see a change in skin colour of the extremity
your cast or splint has a foul odour

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral (adults and adolescents >13 years of age): 50–100 g as a single dose. May give multiple doses for sustained-release preparations or certain toxins like theophylline.
contraindications: Poisoning by hydrocarbons, with high potential for harm if aspirated
risk of gastrointestinal haemorrhage or perforation
side_effects_or_warnings: gastrointestinal obstruction/faecal impaction in dehydrated patients

immediate_actions: Manage airway.
Treat suspected pneumothorax or haemothorax, flail chest, sucking chest wound.
Treat pain.
Send Hb, and type and cross-match.
Call for surgical help.
If haemodynamically unstable (SBP <90 mm Hg) with muffled heart tones, emergent pericardiocentesis.
Obtain chest X-ray.

medical_records.primary_topic: High-risk mechanism of injury / High-risk injuries
clinical_signs: Road traffic accident at speed more than 30 km/hour or with significant damage to vehicle
Thrown from a vehicle or trapped in a vehicle
Pedestrian or cyclist hit by a car
Motorcycle crash with separation of rider from bike
Death of another person in the same accident
Injury from a high- or low-velocity weapon
Penetrating injuries to head, neck, torso, and extremities proximal to elbow and knee
Two or more proximal long-bone fractures
Amputation proximal to wrist or ankle
suspected_conditions: Major trauma requiring admission for observation

medical_records.primary_topic: Calcium gluconate (for Calcium channel blocker overdose)
medical_records.adult_dosing: IV: 0.6 ml/kg (600 mg/kg) to a maximum of 30 ml over 5 minutes; can be repeated every 10–20 minutes, up to 4 doses (using 10% solution).
contraindications: Conditions associated with hypercalcaemia and hypercalciuria (for example, some forms of malignant disease)

medical_records.adult_dosing: IV (loading dose): 2 mg/kg of 1% solution (10 mg/ml) over 5 minutes. Assess after 15 minutes. THEN further dose of 1 mg/kg if no improvement.
contraindications: methaemoglobinaemia due to chlorate or induced by sodium nitrite in treatment of cyanide poisoning

clinical_signs: weakness of the involved muscle groups
pain with passive stretch of the involved muscle
pain out of proportion to the injury
pallor and decreased capillary refill (late finding)

medical_records.adult_dosing: IV: loading dose of 1 g over 10 minutes THEN infusion of 1 g over 8 hours.
side_effects_or_warnings: Rapid intravenous injection may cause dizziness and/or hypotension

equipment_needed: normal saline or clean water for irrigation
step_by_step_guide: Stabilize the patient and assess and treat any life-threatening injuries.
Apply pressure to any active bleeding.
Check and record perfusion distal to the wound.
Examine the wound, explore and remove any foreign body.
Give tetanus toxoid or immunoglobulin for a tetanus-prone wound.
Thoroughly flush the wound with normal saline or clean water (at least 2 litres for contaminated wounds).
Debridement: remove dead tissue until pink, bleeding tissue is visible.
If indicated (clean wounds less than 8 hours old), close the wound with sutures to bring wound edges together.
Use both deep sutures and superficial sutures to reduce tension on the wound.

clinical_signs: Check for obstruction (noisy breathing), wheezing, choking, not able to speak
immediate_actions: If foreign body aspiration, treat choking patient
If suspect anaphylaxis, give 1:1000 epinephrine (adrenaline) IM
Manage airway
Give oxygen 5 litres
If inadequate breathing, assist ventilation with bag valve mask
Help patient assume position of comfort
If wheezing, give salbutamol

clinical_signs: Capillary refill longer than three seconds
SBP <90 mmHg or pulse >110 per minute
immediate_actions: Give oxygen 5 litres if respiratory distress or SpO2 <90
Insert IV, give 1 litre bolus crystalloid (LR or NS) then reassess
Keep warm (cover)
If in second half pregnancy, place on her side (preferably on the left), not on back
If anaphylaxis, give 1:1000 epinephrine (adrenaline) IM
If external bleeding: Apply pressure immediately to stop bleeding
If possible femur fracture - splint
If possible pelvic fracture - apply pelvic binder

medical_records.primary_topic: Altered level of consciousness or convulsing
immediate_actions: Protect from fall or injury
Manage airway and assist into recovery position
Give oxygen 5 litres
Call for help but do not leave patient alone
Give glucose (if blood glucose is low or unknown)
Check (then monitor and record) level of consciousness on AVPU scale
If convulsing: Give diazepam IV or rectally
If convulsing in second half of pregnancy or post-partum up to one week, give magnesium sulfate rather than diazepam

clinical_signs: Severe abdominal pain and abdomen hard on palpation
Severe headache or stiff neck or trauma to head/neck
immediate_actions: Nothing by mouth (NPO)
IV fluids
Give oxygen if respiratory distress or SpO2 <90
Empirical antibiotics IV/IM
Treat pain
If crushing, retrosternal pain and no history of trauma, give aspirin 300 mg chewed, morphine for pain, do ECG

clinical_signs: Any respiratory distress/complaint of difficulty breathing
Violent behaviour toward self or others or very agitated
Bleeding: Large haemoptysis, GI bleeding, External bleeding
New extensive rash with peeling and mucous membrane involvement
Acute pain, cough or dyspnea, priapism, or fever in patient with sickle-cell disease
immediate_actions: Give oxygen 5 litres if SpO2 <90
If wheezing, give salbutamol
If violent behaviour or very agitated, protect, calm, and sedate the patient as appropriate
Measure haemoglobin if any bleeding, pale, weak, fainting, abdominal pain
Repeat Quick Check if in line more than 20 minutes

medical_records.primary_topic: How to help the choking patient
step_by_step_guide: If patient is able to speak or cough: Encourage patient to cough, and observe carefully until obstruction is removed.
If the patient is conscious but not able to speak or cough: Tell patient that you are going to help him or her.

Deliver five abdominal thrusts (if patient is pregnant give chest thrusts): Go behind patient. Have patient standing if possible. Form a fist with one hand and place hand just below the breastbone. Place the other hand over the fist. Pull in and up quickly, using hard thrusts.
If still obstruction, give five back blows.
Repeat abdominal thrusts then back blows until patient speaks or coughs or patient becomes unconscious.
In the unconscious patient: Lie patient on hard surface, open airway, and give two breaths via bag valve mask (BVM), if available.
If you can see foreign body in mouth, manually remove it.
Deliver five abdominal thrusts.

medical_records.adult_dosing: For anaphylaxis: give 1:1000 epinephrine (adrenaline) IM. 0.5 ml if 50 kg or above, 0.4 ml if 40 kg, 0.3 ml if 30 kg. Give IM in anterior lateral thigh. Repeat in five minutes if no response.

step_by_step_guide: Assess Airway: Talk to the patient. Look/listen for snoring, gurgling, stridor, or noisy breathing. Look for foreign body or vomit in mouth.
If Airway Obstructed (No trauma): Position patient on firm surface. Tilt the head. Lift the chin. Remove foreign body if visible. Clear secretions. If unconscious, place in recovery position.
If Airway Obstructed (Trauma): Stabilize cervical spine - do not lift head. Place fingers behind both sides of mandible and lift up (jaw thrust). Clear secretions with suction.

If airway obstructed by tongue, insert airway device: Oropharyngeal airway (if unconscious, slide over tongue) or Nasopharyngeal airway (if semi-conscious, pass well-lubricated airway into one nostril).

Assess Ventilation: If inadequate or patient is cyanotic/unconscious with respiratory distress, assist breathing via bag valve mask. Attach BVM to highest available flow oxygen, place mask over mouth and nose, create seal, squeeze bag to give one breath every 6 seconds.
contraindications: Oropharyngeal airway: If patient resists, gags, or vomits remove immediately.
side_effects_or_warnings: Avoid over-aggressive bagging, as it will result in damage to lungs.

medical_records.adult_dosing: For moderate-severe wheezing: by nebulizer, place 5 mg salbutamol in 5 ml sterile saline driven by oxygen every 10-20 minutes, or continuously. By metered dose inhaler: prime space with 5 puffs, then give 2 puffs via spacer every 2 minutes. For mild wheezing: 100 mcg/puff, give 2 puffs every 20 minutes x 3 times then 2 puffs every 3 to 6 hours.

medical_records.primary_topic: How to insert IV and give fluids rapidly
equipment_needed: Two large bore cannulae (at least 16 or 18 gauge)
step_by_step_guide: If heavy bleeding or shock, insert two large bore cannulae - at least 16 or 18 gauge.
Attach LR or NS. Give one litre as rapidly with infusion wide open.
Assess response of pulse, SBP and signs of perfusion (urine output, mental status).
If still in shock and no evidence of fluid overload, give another bolus.
If still in shock after 2 litres and suspect ongoing blood loss, start blood transfusion and search again for source of bleeding.
Insert urinary catheter and monitor hourly urine output. A urine output of at least 30 ml/hour suggests adequate hydration.
If not able to insert peripheral IV, use alternative: External jugular vein cannulation, Femoral vein cannulation, or Venous cut-down.

medical_records.adult_dosing: If IV inserted: Give naloxone 100 mcg IV - repeat dose until patient RR >10/minute. May be repeated. If no IV: Give naloxone 400 mcg IM or subcutaneous 800 mcg - repeat 2 minutes later if necessary. If patient responded to naloxone for long-acting opioid: Give naloxone IV infusion 0.4 mg/hour (for approximately 12 hours).
side_effects_or_warnings: Giving naloxone can precipitate an opioid withdrawal syndrome that can cause temporary but often significant agitation and discomfort.
Realize that on awakening, the patient may be angry and combative and could injure self or others.
Death can occur if the infusion is interrupted or the patient discharges themselves.

medical_records.adult_dosing: If blood glucose <3 mmol/l (54 mg/dl): Give IV glucose D50 25 to 50 ml or, if D10 available, give 125 to 250 ml rapidly. If no IV glucose is available, give sugar water by mouth (if conscious) or nasogastric tube (dissolve four level teaspoons of sugar in a 200 ml cup of clean water).
Maximum total IV diazepam dose: 30 mg. Typical dose for 50 kg adult: Initial dose 2 ml (10 mg) IV or 4 ml (20 mg) rectally. Second dose after 10 minutes: 1 ml (5 mg) IV or 2 ml (10 mg) rectally.
contraindications: Do not give further diazepam if breathing less than 16 breaths per minute.
side_effects_or_warnings: Respiratory arrest (ventilate with bag valve mask if it develops)

medical_records.primary_topic: Empirical IV/IM antibiotics for emergency management
medical_records.adult_dosing: Give ceftriaxone 1 gm IV or IM (2 gm if suspect meningitis). If ceftriaxone not available, give: ampicillin 2 gm IV or IM AND gentamicin 240 mg IV or IM.

equipment_needed: towel rolls, newspaper, sandbags, or bags of IV fluids
step_by_step_guide: Apply cervical collar or stabilize the neck with locally available material.
Keep the patient lying on a flat surface.
Prevent the neck from moving with locally available materials.
If patient vomits, turn whole patient on their side, keeping head in line with the body.
Keep someone with patient at all times to watch the airway.
To move patient, use log roll technique.

medical_records.primary_topic: How to manage tension pneumothorax or massive haemothorax
step_by_step_guide: Treat tension pneumothorax with emergency needle decompression: insert large bore (#14) cannula along the upper edge of third rib through second intercostal space in mid-clavicular line.
If tension pneumothorax, there will be a rush of expelled air.
Give high flow oxygen.
Call for help from district clinician.
Chest tube should be placed as soon as possible following needle decompression.

medical_records.primary_topic: How to treat sucking chest wound
step_by_step_guide: Give high flow oxygen.
Cover with petroleum gauze.
Tape three sides of the dressing, leaving one side untaped to act as flap valve.
Definitive treatment is to insert chest tube (never insert chest tube through wound).
Debride wound and consider closure.
Place bed sheet under the pelvis.
Pull over the great trochanters/iliac wings - cross over anteriorly.
Pull tight and tie.

medical_records.primary_topic: How to inflate condom over foley catheter to tamponade uterine bleeding
step_by_step_guide: Insert sterile foley catheter up to 3-5 cm below the bulb into a condom.
Tie the condom tightly around the stem of the catheter using sterile gauze ties.
Using a sterile speculum and sponge holding forceps, insert catheter with the condom attached well into the uterine cavity.
Clamp the catheter and leave the end inside the vagina.
Connect a bag of sterile fluid to the end of the catheter (ensuring a tight fit) and allow the fluid to run in and fill the catheter.
Make arrangements for further treatment as appropriate.
Deflate the foley catheter after 24 hours, and if bleeding does not recur remove it.

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initial dose: IM 10 IU or IV infusion 20 IU in 1 litre at 60 drops/min. Continuing dose: IM repeat 10 IU after 20 minutes if heavy bleeding persists, or IV infusion 20 IU in 1 litre at 30 drops/min. MAXIMUM DOSE: Not more that 3 litres of IV fluids containing oxytocin.

medical_records.primary_topic: How to manually remove the placenta
step_by_step_guide: Explain to the woman the need for manual removal of the placenta and obtain her consent.
Insert an IV line.
Give diazepam (10 mg IV) or ketamine sedation if not comatose.
Ensure the bladder is empty. Catheterize if necessary.
Wash hands and forearms well and put on long sterile gloves.
With the left hand, hold the umbilical cord with the clamp. Then pull the cord gently until it is horizontal.
Insert right hand into the vagina and up into the uterus.
Leave the cord and hold the fundus with the left hand in order to support the fundus of the uterus and to provide counter-traction during removal.
Detach the placenta from the implantation site by keeping the fingers tightly together and using the edge of the hand to gradually make a space.
Withdraw the right hand from the uterus gradually, bringing the placenta with it.
Explore the inside of the uterine cavity to ensure all placental tissue has been removed.
Examine the uterine surface of the placenta to ensure that lobes and membranes are complete.
contraindications: If hours or days have passed since delivery, or if the placenta is retained due to constriction ring or closed cervix (DO NOT persist)
If the placenta does not separate from the uterine surface by gentle sideways movement (suspect placenta accreta, DO NOT persist)
side_effects_or_warnings: Inversion of the uterus (prevent by providing counter-traction to the fundus)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Give IV and IM combined dose (loading dose): Insert IV line and give fluids slowly. Give 4 g of magnesium sulfate (20 ml of 20% solution) IV slowly over 20 minutes. AND Give 10 g of magnesium sulfate IM: give 5 g (10 ml of 50% solution) IM deep in upper outer quadrant of each buttock with 1 ml of 2% lidocaine. If convulsions recur after 15 minutes, give an additional 2 g IV.
contraindications: DO NOT give the next dose if: knee jerk absent, urine output <100 ml/4 hours, or RR <16/minute.
side_effects_or_warnings: Respiratory failure or death (if injected too rapidly)
Antidote: calcium gluconate 1 g IV over 10 minutes.

medical_records.adult_dosing: Pretreat with midazolam 0.05 mg/kg IV over 2 minutes or diazepam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg IV. Sedate: give ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV over 2 minutes. Repeat 0.5 mg/kg IV every 10 minutes as needed. Alternative to IV: give 4 mg/kg IM.

equipment_needed: Endotracheal tube (6.0-7.0 in females, 7.0-8.0 in males)
step_by_step_guide: Give high flow oxygen via BVM or face mask with reservoir before the procedure.
Position patient in sniffing position (place pillow under neck if no trauma).
Give sedation if required (midazolam or ketamine).
Open the patient's mouth by separating the lips and pulling on the upper jaw with the index finger.
Hold a laryngoscope in the left hand, insert it into the mouth directed to the right tonsil. Sweep to the midline, keeping the tongue on the left to bring the epiglottis into view.
Advance the blade until the angle between the base of the tongue and the epiglottis is reached.
Lift laryngoscope up and away from you at a 45 degree angle to bring the vocal cords into view.
Take the endotracheal tube in the right hand and insert it into the mouth through the cords so the cuff rests just below the cords.
Inflate the cuff to provide a minimal leak when the bag is squeezed.
Attach tube to bag connected to high flow oxygen.
Confirm placement (listen over lung fields/stomach, look for condensation, chest rise).
contraindications: No post-intubation plan (invasive mechanical ventilator unavailable, except for short-term reversible conditions or planned transfer)
side_effects_or_warnings: Right mainstem bronchus intubation (pull ETT out in small increments)

step_by_step_guide: Hyperextend the neck (unless known or suspected C-spine injury).
Clean the area and infiltrate with local anaesthetic.
Incise through the skin vertically with a 1.5 cm cut and use blunt dissection to ensure that you can see the membrane between the thyroid and cricoid.
With a #22 or #23 scalpel blade, stab through the membrane into the hollow trachea.
Rotate the blade 90 degrees, insert a curved artery forceps alongside the blade, remove the blade and open the forceps side to side, widening the space.
Pass a thin introducer or a nasogastric tube into the trachea if very small access.
Run a 4-6 endotracheal tube over the introducer and pass it into the trachea.
Remove the introducer, if used.

clinical_signs: History of previous wheezing episodes associated with chest tightness
Identifiable triggers (upper respiratory infection, allergen exposure, exercise, cold air, extreme emotion)
Breathing or cough commonly worse at night
Absent breath sounds and use of neck muscles in severe attacks

step_by_step_guide: Classify asthma severity based on frequency of symptoms and peak flow or FEV1 (Intermittent, Mild persistent, Moderate persistent, Severe persistent).
For intermittent asthma, prescribe inhaled salbutamol as needed.
For mild persistent asthma, prescribe inhaled salbutamol as needed and add low-dose inhaled corticosteroids (e.g., Beclometasone 100-250 mcg twice daily).
For moderate persistent asthma, increase inhaled corticosteroids and consider adding a long-acting beta-agonist.
For severe persistent asthma, maximize inhaled therapies, consider oral prednisolone, and refer to specialist care.
Counsel patients to stop smoking and avoid known triggers.

clinical_signs: Symptoms of chronic cough and progressive shortness of breath
History of tobacco smoking, occupational exposures, or exposure to biomass fuel smoke
Fast respiratory rate and reduced breath sounds throughout all lung fields
Use of accessory muscles to breathe (advanced stages)
Clinical signs of right heart failure such as engorged jugular veins and bilateral leg oedema

step_by_step_guide: Classify COPD severity based on FEV1 (% predicted) and symptoms (Mild, Moderate, Severe, Very severe).
Counsel all patients to stop smoking and avoid indoor air pollution.
Administer pneumococcal and annual influenza vaccinations.
For mild COPD, prescribe short-acting bronchodilator (salbutamol or ipratropium) as needed.
For moderate COPD, use short-acting bronchodilator as needed plus long-acting beta-agonist (LABA) or long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA).
For severe COPD, add inhaled corticosteroids (beclometasone) to the LABA/LAMA regimen.
Refer for specialist care for severe or very severe COPD to consider long-term home oxygen therapy.

clinical_signs: Oedema (facial, upper extremity, or generalized anasarca)
Shortness of breath or pulmonary oedema
Nausea, vomiting, decreased appetite, or weight loss
Signs of uraemia: pericarditis, altered consciousness, neuropathy, bleeding, uraemic frost, uraemic foetor, uraemic flap
Oliguria (less than 400 ml urine output in 24 hours)

step_by_step_guide: Estimate the patient's Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) using the Cockcroft-Gault formula to stage the CKD.
Look for and treat reversible causes such as infectious and autoimmune conditions, hypovolaemia, and urinary tract obstructions.
Stop the administration of potentially nephrotoxic drugs including NSAIDs, aminoglycoside antibiotics, and IV contrast media.
Reduce sodium intake to <2.4 g/day or <100 mmol/day.
Ensure careful blood sugar control; treat diabetics with ACE inhibitors to slow progression.
Maintain aggressive blood pressure control (lower than 130/80), preferring ACE inhibitors especially in patients with proteinuria.
Treat hyperlipidaemia with a statin.
Encourage smoking cessation.
Manage volume overload using furosemide, adjusting doses based on the degree of renal impairment.
Refer end-stage patients for peritoneal dialysis or haemodialysis to higher centres.

clinical_signs: Gradual onset over months to years
Deficits in intellectual domains in the absence of an alteration in consciousness
Difficulty in performing automatic tasks (e.g. daily routines)
Unsteady gait, loss of balance, or impaired hand-eye coordination

step_by_step_guide: Ensure the patient's safety with an appropriate environment and adequate level of supervision.
Ensure that the patient is taking medications as prescribed.
Protect wandering patients and supervise any cigarette smoking.
Implement strategies to prevent falls (e.g. reduce floor clutter, re-evaluate orthostatic medications, encourage tailored physical activity).
Apply principles of reality orientation, cognitive stimulation, and reminiscence therapy.
Maintain familiar objects and people, emphasize routines, and provide proper day/night lighting.
Provide a clock and calendar in the room to orient the patient.
Perform regular medical reviews at least every 3-6 months to assess underlying precipitants of behavioural symptoms.
Avoid unnecessary antipsychotics; if required for severe agitation or hallucinations, use the lowest possible dose of haloperidol (e.g. 0.5 mg) and titrate slowly.
Avoid benzodiazepines (like diazepam) as they increase confusion.

medical_records.primary_topic: Sad or Low Mood including Depression
clinical_signs: Sad or low mood persisting for at least 2 weeks
Fatigue, loss of energy, or tiredness
Loss of interest or pleasure in daily activities
Suicidal thoughts, acts, or recurrent thoughts of death
Unexplained somatic symptoms (aches, pains, tingling, shortness of breath, GI distress)
suspected_conditions: Depression secondary to medical conditions (e.g. hypothyroidism) or medications

step_by_step_guide: Perform a comprehensive assessment including suicide risk; ensure patient safety.
Identify and manage any underlying medical conditions contributing to the mood state.
For mild depression, provide psychotherapy and counselling; do not offer antidepressants initially.
For moderate to severe depression, prescribe Fluoxetine or a tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) such as Amitriptyline.
Start antidepressants at a low dose and gradually increase; monitor closely for agitation and suicidal ideation, especially during the early phase of treatment.
Encourage physical activity and behavioural activation.
Provide psycho-education and support for the patient and their family.
Do not consider ending antidepressant treatment before 9-12 months after recovery; when terminating, taper the dose slowly to avoid withdrawal symptoms.

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 20 mg daily. May start at 10 mg daily (or 20 mg every other day) to reduce side-effects, then increase to 20 mg as tolerated. Max dose 60 mg daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: Increased risk for agitation and suicidal ideation and behaviour
Can precipitate mania in patients with bipolar disorder

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 50 mg at bedtime (25 mg in elderly or medically ill). Increase by 25-50 mg every 1-2 weeks, aiming for 100-150 mg by 4-6 weeks. Maximum dose 200 mg daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: Highly lethal in overdose (dispense small supplies if suicide risk is high)
Cardiac arrhythmias (do not use in patients with recent myocardial infarction)

home_care_instructions: Set clear goals for reduced drinking, such as no more than 2 standard drinks a day with at least 2 alcohol-free days per week.
Maintain a drink diary to monitor alcohol consumption.
Use local community-based organizations, self-help groups, and peer support for ongoing motivation.
Recognize and avoid environments or social situations that trigger the urge to drink heavily.
Improve nutrition and take multivitamin supplements, specifically thiamine, to protect brain function.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Experiencing signs of severe alcohol withdrawal such as anxiety, severe nausea, hand tremors, sweating, confusion, or seizures.
Inability to control drinking despite intention to stop.
Developing jaundice or severe abdominal pain.

clinical_signs: Pain on weight-bearing or activity that is eased by rest

step_by_step_guide: Advise weight loss if the patient is overweight to reduce load on joints.
Prescribe physical therapy and regular gentle exercise.
Provide pain control starting with paracetamol.
If paracetamol is insufficient, prescribe NSAIDs (e.g. ibuprofen), monitoring for gastrointestinal side-effects.

clinical_signs: Sudden, unexplained swelling and severe pain at the base of the big toe (podagra)
Redness, hot, tender, and swollen joint

step_by_step_guide: Treat acute flares with NSAIDs and colchicine.
Consider corticosteroids if renal insufficiency prevents the use of NSAIDs/colchicine.
For chronic management, prescribe urate-lowering agents and low-dose colchicine.
Provide preventative advice: decrease meat and fish intake, encourage weight loss, and advise absolute avoidance of alcohol.

medical_records.primary_topic: Chronic Pain Management in Palliative Care
step_by_step_guide: Assess pain regularly using body diagrams and numeric pain intensity or FACES scales.
Administer analgesics by mouth whenever possible.
Give pain medications 'by the clock' (at fixed time intervals) rather than purely PRN, to prevent pain from returning.
Follow the WHO 3-step analgesic ladder: Step 1 (Non-opioids like paracetamol/ibuprofen), Step 2 (Weak opioids like codeine added to non-opioids), Step 3 (Strong opioids like oral morphine).
Titrate morphine doses carefully against the patient's pain; there is no ceiling dose for morphine.
Always prescribe a laxative alongside opioids to prevent severe constipation.
Integrate non-pharmacological therapies such as psychosocial support, relaxation, massage, heat/cold packs, and spiritual counseling.
Use adjuvant medications for neuropathic pain, such as low-dose amitriptyline or carbamazepine.
Cleanse the area of the biopsy with skin antiseptic.
Anaesthetize the area with 1–2% lidocaine.
If flat, inject anaesthetic or saline under the lesion to raise it slightly.
Hold the scalpel parallel to the skin and begin. Complete the incision in one stroke. The aim is to take only a specimen of superficial tissue.

If done for the diagnosis of cutaneous leishmaniasis, the slit-skin technique should be used. Incise several millimetres outward from the active border of a lesion, making sure to go deep enough to penetrate the dermis. This should be followed by a scrape.
Dress the wound with simple dry gauze dressing.
contraindications: Do not perform shave biopsy of pigmented lesions – melanoma is more difficult to stage if shaved.

step_by_step_guide: Cleanse the area of the biopsy with skin antiseptic.
Anaesthetize the area with 1–2% lidocaine.
Stretch the skin perpendicular to the Langer’s lines (natural creases in the skin).
Hold the cylindrical knife (trephine) perpendicular to the skin and gently push downward while rotating it clockwise and counter clockwise to cut through the skin.
Use a forceps or needle to lift the specimen, and cut it free from the underlying tissue below the dermis.
side_effects_or_warnings: Avoid squeezing the specimen with a haemostat or forceps to avoid crush artefact.

medical_records.primary_topic: Skin snip for the diagnosis of microfilariasis
step_by_step_guide: Select the sites with the highest numbers of microfilariae for examination (e.g., over the scapula, iliac crest, or calf).
Clean the skin with antiseptic and allow it to dry.
Insert a fine sterile needle almost horizontally into the skin and raise the point of the needle, lifting with it a small piece of skin measuring about 2 mm in diameter and height.
Cut off the piece of skin with a sterile razor blade or scalpel.
Disinfect all instruments used during the procedure.
Place the tissue sample on a microscope slide with a few drops of saline or water, cover with cover slip, and send to the laboratory immediately.

equipment_needed: mask for the health worker if TB is suspected
step_by_step_guide: Clean the skin with antiseptic.
Fix the lymph node or mass so that it will not move.
Enter the lymph node parallel to the fingers of the left hand.
Apply gentle suction syringe by pulling back the plunger 2–3 ml.
The mass is entered and multiple, sequential passes are made without exiting the skin surface.
Release the syringe completely and exit the skin.
Place a small drop of aspirated fluid on a glass slide.
A smear is made by laying another glass slide on top of the drop of fluid and pulling the slides apart to spread the fluid.

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
step_by_step_guide: A general anaesthetic may be required as lymph nodes are located beneath the fascia.
Make an incision along the skin lines and dissect through the subcutaneous tissue, while controlling any bleeding.
Identify the lymph node with a fingertip and incise the overlying superficial fascia.
Dissect the node from surrounding tissue without directly grasping it.
Grasp the attached adventitial tissue with a small artery forceps, or place a figure-of-8 suture into the node for traction.
Separate all the tissue attached to the node.
Control the hilar vessels with forceps and ligate them with absorbable suture after the node has been removed.

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
bone marrow aspiration needle with removable stylet and 1–2 ml syringe
step_by_step_guide: Position the patient lying face down or lying on the side opposite to that where the procedure will be done.
Identify the site, usually three finger widths from the midline and two finger widths below the posterior iliac crest, and cleanse with antiseptic.
Anaesthetize the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and periosteum using lidocaine.
Make a small 3 mm incision at the site.

Aspiration: Insert the bone marrow aspiration needle perpendicular to the skin. Turn needle toward anterior superior iliac spine upon hitting periosteum, twist into marrow cavity, remove stylet, and aspirate max 0.5 ml at a time.

Biopsy: Using the same incision, insert the biopsy needle at a slightly different spot. Twist until lodged firmly, remove stylet, advance 15–20 mm, change direction to sever sample, and remove needle.
contraindications: low platelets (<20 x 109/litre) may require a platelet transfusion
skin infection or osteomyelitis near the chosen site

equipment_needed: 3–5% acetic acid or Lugol’s iodine solution
step_by_step_guide: Begin by performing a speculum exam.
Adjust the light source in order to get the best view of the cervix.
Use a cotton swab to remove any discharge, blood, or mucus from the cervix.
Identify the squamocolumnar junction (SCJ), and the area around it.
Apply acetic acid or Lugol’s iodine to the cervix; wait a minute or two to allow colour changes to develop.
Inspect the SCJ carefully, and be sure to visualize all of it. Look for any raised and thickened white plaques or acetowhite epithelium (or saffron-yellow areas if iodine used).
Use a fresh swab to remove any remaining acetic acid or iodine solution from the cervix and vagina.
Gently remove the speculum.
contraindications: Not recommended for use in postmenopausal women, because their transition zone is most often inside the endocervical canal and not visible on speculum exam.

step_by_step_guide: Carry out the procedure during the patient’s premenstrual phase.
Perform a bimanual exam to determine the size of the uterus and direction of the cervix.
Perform a speculum examination and cleanse the cervical os with iodine.
Grasp the cervix with a toothed tenaculum and pass a uterine sound to determine the size of the uterus. If sound cannot be passed, perform cervical block with lidocaine.
If the sound still cannot be passed, attempt to dilate the cervix using narrow metal dilators.
Insert an endometrial biopsy curette and obtain at least 4 pieces of the endometrium for histopathological examination.

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
10 ml syringe filled with water or saline
step_by_step_guide: Position the patient lying face up with knees bent and apart.
Put on sterile gloves and apply antiseptic to the labia and urethra. Isolate the area with a perforated sterile towel.
Check the integrity of the urinary catheter balloon, and then lubricate the catheter with a generous amount of sterile liquid paraffin or lidocaine jelly.
Gently insert the urinary catheter into the urethra, located just at the top of the vaginal opening.
Insert at least 20 cm of the catheter to ensure that it is in the bladder.
Inflate the balloon with 10–15 ml of sterile water or clean urine. Partially withdraw the catheter until its balloon abuts on the bladder neck.
Secure the catheter to the patient’s thigh using tape and connect to a closed system sterile container.
contraindications: possible fracture of the pubic symphysis (demonstrated by blood at the urethral opening after trauma)

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
10 ml syringe filled with water or saline
step_by_step_guide: Position the patient lying face up.
Wash the area with soap and water, retracting the foreskin. Put on sterile gloves, apply antiseptic to the urethra and glans.
Check the balloon integrity and lubricate the catheter with sterile liquid paraffin or lidocaine jelly.
Hold the penis vertically and slightly stretched with one hand, and introduce the urinary catheter gently.
If resistance is felt at 12–15 cm, angle it down to allow it to enter the posterior urethra.
Urine escaping confirms entry. Advance the catheter 5–10 cm before inflating the balloon.
Pull the foreskin back over the glans once the catheter has been placed to prevent paraphimosis.
Inflate the balloon with 10–15 ml of sterile water, partially withdraw until the balloon abuts the bladder neck.
Strap the penis and catheter laterally to the abdominal wall to prevent compression ulceration.

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
10 ml syringe filled with water or saline
step_by_step_guide: Assess the extent of bladder distension by inspection and palpation.
Clean the area with antiseptic.
Raise a weal of local anaesthetic in the midline, 2 cm above the symphysis pubis, and then continue with deeper infiltration.
Make a simple puncture 2 cm above the symphysis pubis in the midline with a 16-gauge needle attached to a 50 ml syringe while aspirating to find urine.
Introduce the trochar and cannula and advance them vertically with care.
Introduce the catheter well into the bladder. Once urine flows freely, withdraw the cannula and inflate the catheter balloon.
Fix the catheter to the skin with a stitch and connect it to a bag or bottle.
contraindications: Caution should be taken in patients with previous abdominal surgeries due to adhesions putting them at greater risk for bowel injury.
side_effects_or_warnings: Leakage of urine into the abdomen

step_by_step_guide: Perform a bimanual examination and speculum examination to assess eligibility.
Clean the cervix and vagina with appropriate antiseptic.
Slowly insert the tenaculum through the speculum and close it just enough to gently hold the cervix.
Pass the uterine sound through the cervix to measure the depth and position of the uterus.
Load the IUD into the inserter while both are still in the unopened sterile package.
Insert the IUD and then remove the inserter.
Cut the strings on the IUD, leaving about 3 centimetres hanging out of the cervix.
contraindications: recent, untreated puerperal sepsis or septic abortion
between 48 hours and 4 weeks since giving birth

equipment_needed: lidocaine without epinephrine, 5–10 ml syringe, 23- to 25-gauge needle
step_by_step_guide: Sedate the patient if necessary – consider ketamine.
Cleanse the skin of the genitalia with antiseptic.
Isolate the penis with a perforated towel and inject lidocaine in a ring around its base.
Take hold of the oedematous part of the penis in the fist and squeeze firmly until the oedema fluid passes proximally under the constricting band.
Pull the foreskin over the glans.
If manual reduction fails, perform a phimotic ring incision. Infiltrate proximally to distally through the constricting ring and incise slowly.
If necessary, perform a dorsal slit by clamping the foreskin with 2 artery forceps and incising between them.

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
step_by_step_guide: Clean the area with antiseptic and cover with sterile drapes.
Anaesthetize the skin and subcutaneous tissue with lidocaine.
Place a tourniquet proximal to the cutdown site to visualize the vein.
Incise the skin perpendicular to the vein using a scalpel.
Isolate and mobilize the vein using blunt dissection.
Pass two sutures under the vein proximal and distal to the cannulation site.
Tie the distal suture.
Incise the vein at a 45° angle between the two sutures.
Cannulate the vein with the IV catheter using the venous dilator.
Tie the proximal suture around the vein and the catheter to hold it in place.
Remove the tourniquet and close the incision.
contraindications: Should not be performed if less invasive means of obtaining venous access are available.

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
step_by_step_guide: Position the patient lying face up on the examination table.
Palpate the superolateral or superomedial aspect of the patella and mark a spot 1 cm superior and lateral to this point.
Cleanse the area with skin antiseptic and anaesthetize.
Steady the patella with one hand.
Insert a 21-gauge needle at a 45° angle to the knee, aiming for below the patella.
Aspirate fluid once the needle has penetrated more than a few centimetres.
Withdraw the needle and send fluid for studies.
contraindications: significant overlying cellulitis or soft tissue infection

step_by_step_guide: Have the patient bend her or his left arm to a 90 degree angle. Locate the top of the shoulder and the elbow bone.
Using a string between the top of the shoulder and elbow, find the mid of the upper arm and mark with a pen.
With the patient's arm relaxed and resting at her or his side, wrap MUAC tape around the arm at the pen mark.
There should not be any space between the patient's skin and the tape, but avoid wrapping the tape too tight.
Read the MUAC in mm from middle window exactly where the arrows point inward. Record the MUAC to the nearest 1 mm (0.1 cm).

equipment_needed: Ophthalmoscope with a blue filter or strong white light
step_by_step_guide: Moisten a fluorescein-impregnated strip with a drop of saline or artificial tears.
Touch the strip to the inside of the lower lid.
Illuminate the cornea with an ophthalmoscope with a blue filter if available (if not available, a strong white light may also reveal defects).
Look for epithelial defects that stain bright green.

medical_records.primary_topic: Fundoscopy (Examination of the posterior segment of the eye)
step_by_step_guide: Perform the examination in a suitably darkened room.
The patient's right eye should be examined with the examiner's right eye, and vice versa.
Ask the patient to focus on an object in the distance, and keep the examiner's head vertical to permit this.
Turn to the +10DS lens in the lens wheel and observe the eye from 10 cm.
Slowly move closer to the patient and at the same time gradually reduce the power of the lens in the wheel and focus on the crystalline lens, the vitreous, and finally the fundus.
Once a blood vessel on the fundus has been located, move along it and locate the optic disc. Consider its colour, its margins, and the cup if there is one.
Examine the retinal background in each quadrant.
To examine the macula and the fovea, instruct the patient to look into the ophthalmoscope light.
Treat promptly as it can lead to serious complication, such as skin loss, infection, and in extreme cases loss of the penis.
Reduce oedema by wrapping the swollen area in gauze and applying increasing pressure on the gauze to squeeze the tissue fluid out. This may take 10-15 minutes.
Once fluid is out, it is usually possible to replace the foreskin over the glans.
If the above is unsuccessful, surgical intervention is indicated (dorsal slit or circumcision).
side_effects_or_warnings: If ischaemia of the glans is present, proceed to surgical interventions or involve an urologist for urgent management.

medical_records.primary_topic: First Aid for Workplace HIV Exposure
step_by_step_guide: For a needle-stick or laceration: Attend to the injury immediately. Wash site immediately using soap and water or a mild disinfectant solution.
If running water is not available, clean site with a gel or hand-rub solution.
For a splash that contacts unbroken skin: Wash the area immediately.

For a splash that contacts the eye: Sit in a chair, tilt the head back, and have a colleague gently pour water or NS over the eye, pulling eyelids up and down to make sure the eye is cleaned thoroughly.
If contact lenses are worn, leave them in place while irrigating the eye. Once the eye has been cleaned, remove the contact lenses and clean them in the normal manner.
For a splash that contacts the mouth: Spit the fluid out immediately. Rinse the mouth thoroughly, using water or saline, and spit again. Repeat this process several times.
Contact the health centre contact person designated for workplace HIV exposure to determine if PEP is needed.
contraindications: Do not squeeze the injury site.
Do not use any strong solutions, such as bleach or iodine, as these may irritate the wound or skin.
Do not use soap or disinfectant on the eye or in the mouth.

medical_records.primary_topic: Morphine IV Infusion for Refractory Severe Acute Pain
equipment_needed: IV line with a metal gate-clamp
step_by_step_guide: Ensure a strictly controlled rate, using a metal gate-clamp in the IV rather than a roller device (which can become loose), and monitor closely.
Give a bolus dose of 2 mg initially.
Follow with a continuous infusion at 1 mg/hour.
Titrate the infusion hourly as needed for pain control, usually between 5-35 mg/hour.
Slow the infusion if the patient begins to show signs of somnolence.
Stop the infusion if there is respiratory depression (RR <16) or hypotension (SBP <90).
If systolic BP <90 or falls more than 20 mm Hg, SpO2 <90, or respiratory rate <10/minute, determine if patient needs more fluids and give naloxone.
Assess volume status and hydrate appropriately.
Perform rapid cooling by spraying naked patient with a mist of lukewarm water while a fan or cool breeze is used to blow air over the moist skin (evaporative cooling).
Give IV diazepam (5 mg IV) to suppress shivering induced by cooling.
Give an antipyretic for infectious causes.
Continuously monitor core temperature with a rectal probe to monitor for response to cooling therapy. If a rectal probe is unavailable, frequently monitor oral temperature.
Stop cooling therapy once the temperature is 38 to 39 C to avoid excessively low body temperatures.
contraindications: Do not use antipyretic agents for heat stroke, intracranial injury, or drug-induced hyperthermia.
side_effects_or_warnings: Excessively low body temperatures if cooling is not stopped in time

step_by_step_guide: Immediately scrub with alkaline soap and water, and flush with water for 15 minutes.
Apply povidone-iodine or benzalkonium chloride 1-4% on the wound, if available.
Categorize the type of contact with the rabid animal (Category I, II, or III) to determine post-exposure vaccination and immunoglobulin needs.
Verify if the patient has ascites WITH or WITHOUT peripheral oedema.
For patients WITH peripheral oedema, mobilize a high fluid volume (2 litres/day) by paracentesis without developing intravascular depletion and azotaemia.
For patients WITHOUT peripheral oedema, restrict fluid removal to a rate that does not exceed 500 ml/day.
For patients with diuretic-resistant ascites, perform serial large volume therapeutic paracenteses, not exceeding 4-5 litres.
In exceptional cases where massive ascites cause severe shortness of breath, a large volume (4-5 litres) therapeutic paracentesis may be done for control of symptoms.
contraindications: Do not exceed a rate of fluid removal of 500 ml/day in patients with ascites but without oedema.

medical_records.primary_topic: Nerve Blocks for Specific Severe Pains
step_by_step_guide: Inject anaesthetic around a nerve to alleviate pain in the distribution of that nerve.
For shoulder surgeries and procedures: Use a brachial plexus - interscalene block.
For procedures below the knee: Use a sciatic nerve block.
For femoral shaft fractures and knee procedures: Use a femoral nerve block.
For refractory neuropathic pain resulting from herpes zoster: Treat with a block of the relevant thoracic (or other affected) nerve.
side_effects_or_warnings: Nerve blocks can be technically difficult procedures that should be done by experienced clinicians.

medical_records.primary_topic: Evacuation of the Uterus for Septic Abortion
equipment_needed: Foley catheter with a 50 ml balloon (if standard means unavailable)
step_by_step_guide: Stabilize the patient and start antibiotics prior to the procedure.
Perform prompt evacuation of the uterus using vacuum aspiration (recommended for up to 12-14 weeks) under local anaesthesia with minimal sedation.
Vacuum aspiration should not be routinely completed by sharp curettage.
If gestational age is after 12-14 weeks, refer to an experienced provider for dilatation and evacuation. If unavailable, use misoprostol.
If none of these means are available, place a Foley catheter with a 50 ml balloon attached in the lower uterus.
Apply one kilogram of traction from an orthopaedic weight at the foot of the bed to the catheter, which dilates the cervix and stimulates contractions.
side_effects_or_warnings: Uncontrolled infection (ongoing sepsis, severe infection with gas-producing clostridium species) which may require a total hysterectomy.

medical_records.primary_topic: Applying a Pressure Bandage for Venous Ulcers
step_by_step_guide: Elevate the legs as often as possible to promote venous return.
Apply an elastocrepe bandage starting from the ankle to the knee.
Apply higher compression to the foot.
Decrease compression as the bandage approaches the knee.

home_care_instructions: Encourage frequent intake of oral fluids such as water, diluted tea, fruit juices.
Wipe the body with a damp cloth (tepid sponge) or give a lukewarm bath.
Encourage the patient to wear only light clothes.
Give paracetamol, aspirin, or ibuprofen to reduce the fever (no more than 4 g of paracetamol in 24 hours).
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: The fever does not improve or comes back after treatment.
The fever is accompanied by a cough, diarrhoea, severe pain, confusion, night sweats, rigors, stiff neck or change in consciousness.
A woman has fever in pregnancy, after birth, or after abortion (spontaneous or not).

home_care_instructions: The patient and all close contacts must be treated simultaneously, including the entire household and sexual partners, even if they are asymptomatic.
Clothing or bed linen used by the patient should be washed and dried well (or dry-cleaned).
Do not bathe before applying the treatment (this increases systemic absorption and does not help).

Apply the cream to the whole skin surface, giving particular attention to the flexures, genitalia, intergluteal cleft, between the fingers and under the fingernails. Include the face, neck and scalp, but avoid areas near the eyes and mouth.
The cream may irritate the skin a little, especially if there are excoriations.
Keep the cream on for the treatment period: overnight treatment for 3 days.
If any cream is washed off during the treatment period (e.g. hands), reapply immediately.
Wash the cream off at the end of the treatment period.
Itching should start to diminish within a few days, but may persist for a number of weeks. This does not mean that the treatment has failed.
If the affected person has dry skin, moisturize with aqueous cream or petroleum jelly mixed with water.
Use 1 spoon of oil (bath or vegetable) in the bath water when washing.
Apply diluted chlorhexidine (0.05%) after a bath.
Rub the itchy skin with local remedies (examples: effective and safe herbs, cucumber, or wet tea bags or leaves put in a clean piece of cloth and soaked in hot water).
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Seek help from a trained health worker for painful blisters or extensive skin infection.

home_care_instructions: For small sores, clean gently with salty water and allow to dry.
If painful, use paracetamol or aspirin.
For deep or large sores, clean daily with diluted salt water and cover with a clean, light dressing to encourage healing.
Make sure that the patient does not lie on pressure sores.
Use foam pads or pillows or water beds to take pressure off the sore.
Use pillows to keep the knees and ankles apart.
Change the patient's position at least every 2 hours.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: A bad smell from the sore.
Redness or warmth around the sore.
Any discoloured skin, or bedsores that are getting worse.

medical_records.primary_topic: Home Care for Nausea or Vomiting
home_care_instructions: Drink plenty of fluids.
Eat favourite, available foods that cause less nausea.
Eat frequent, small portions of food.
Use effective and safe local remedies, e.g. licking ash from wood.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Blood in vomit, dehydration, headache or stiff neck, severe abdominal pain.

medical_records.primary_topic: Treatment of Diarrhoea at Home (Plan A)
home_care_instructions: Drink extra fluid: as much as the patient will take.
Ensure safe water for the patient - boiled or disinfected.
Give the patient frequent drinks in small amounts, such as rice soup, porridge, water (with food), other soups or oral rehydration solution (ORS), but avoid sweet drinks.
Drink at least 200-300 ml after each loose stool and continue drinking extra fluid until the diarrhoea stops.
If ORS is provided: teach the patient how to mix and drink ORS and give 2 packets to take home.
If the patient is vomiting, they should continue to take small sips.
The patient should continue eating.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: The patient is vomiting and has fever.
There is blood in the stool.
The diarrhoea continues more than 5 days.
There is broken skin around the rectal area.

home_care_instructions: After the sick person has passed a stool: clean with soft tissue paper.
Wash the rectal area when necessary with soap and water.
Apply petroleum jelly around the rectal area.
The person should then sit in basin of water with a pinch of salt. If this is comfortable, suggest doing it twice daily.
Provide adequate support and reassurance about the situation.
Pelvic floor muscle exercises - help strengthen urinary sphincter and pelvic floor muscles.
Scheduled toilet trips - going to the toilet according to the clock rather than waiting for the need to go, usually every 2-4 hours.
Use pads and protective garments (absorbent pads) to help manage urine loss.
Adult diapers are sometimes available in both disposable and reusable forms.
Consider wearing plastic underwear over regular underwear to help keep dry; others opt for washable underwear and briefs with waterproof panels.
The patient should rest.
The patient should try self-steaming, using a basin with hot water (but warn them to be careful of being burned by the water).
The patient should take an over-the-counter pain medicine, e.g. paracetamol.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: The headache is not responding to treatment.

medical_records.primary_topic: Preventing Malnutrition and Promoting Weight Gain
home_care_instructions: Encourage the sick person to eat, but do not use force as the body may not be able to accept it, and the patient may vomit.
Offer smaller, attractive meals of what the sick person likes more frequently.
Let the sick person choose the foods she or he desires to eat from what is available.
Encourage the patient to eat nutrient-dense foods that are locally available.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: The sick person consistently refuses to eat any food or is not able to swallow.

medical_records.primary_topic: Criteria for Discharge in Severe Malnutrition
home_care_instructions: Severely malnourished adolescents and adults can be discharged when they are eating well and gaining weight.
Ensure they have a reliable source of nutritious food outside the hospital.
Ensure any other health problems have been diagnosed and treatment has begun.
Adults should continue to receive a supplemented diet as outpatients until their BMI is >18.5; for adolescents, their diets should be supplemented until their BMI-for-age is >5th percentile.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Failure to respond to treatment or refusal to follow the treatment regimen.

medical_records.primary_topic: Difficulty breathing not due to upper airway obstruction
clinical_signs: Very fast or very slow respiratory rates
Use of accessory muscles to breathe
suspected_conditions: Pulmonary oedema (fluid overload, heart failure, renal failure)
Obstructive shock (tamponade, pulmonary emboli, tension pneumothorax)

clinical_signs: Signs of volume overload: distended neck veins, hepatomegaly, ascites, lower-extremity oedema
History of cardiomyopathy or kidney disease
suspected_conditions: Acute lung injury (severe malaria, severe pneumonia, severe sepsis, poisoning, acute pancreatitis, tocolytic medication/pre-eclampsia)

step_by_step_guide: Ask the woman to empty her bladder (urinate) and remove her underwear. Position the woman on the examination table.
External genital exam: look for redness, lumps, swelling, unusual discharge, sores, tears, and scars around the genitals.
Speculum exam: Hold the speculum blades together sideways and insert them into the vagina.
When the speculum is halfway in, turn it so the handle is down. Gently open the blades and look for the cervix.
Tighten the screw to lock the speculum. Check the cervix for cysts, redness, discharge, ulcers, sores, or abnormal growths.
Gently pull the speculum until the blades are clear of the cervix, allow blades to close, and remove.
Bimanual exam: Put pointing and middle finger of a gloved hand in the vagina. Palpate the cervix for firmness and cervical motion tenderness.
Use the fingers in the vagina to move pelvic organs toward the abdomen to palpate the womb, tubes, and ovaries with the other hand.
Ask the woman to cough or push down to check for prolapse.
Begin by performing a speculum exam.
Insert the long tip of the wooden spatula or brush into the os, and rotate it through a full circle (360 degrees).
Smear both sides of the spatula or brush onto a glass slide with one or two careful swipes.
Sample any abnormalities outside the cervical os, and smear on another slide.
Immediately fix each slide using spray fixative or immerse in 95% ethanol for at least 5 minutes.
Gently close and remove the speculum.

medical_records.primary_topic: Colposcopy, cervical biopsy, and endocervical curettage
step_by_step_guide: Pain from cervical biopsies can be reduced by having the patient take paracetamol or ibuprofen 1–2 hours prior.
Inspect the cervix at low-power magnification (5X to 10X). Identify the transformation zone and the squamocolumnar junction (SCJ).
Apply saline to the cervix. Inspect with a green filter and 15X magnification for abnormal vascular patterns.
Apply acetic acid. Wait 1 or 2 minutes to allow colour changes to develop.
Integrate the findings of the saline test and acetic acid test.
Take cervical biopsies of the most abnormal areas.
If necessary, perform endocervical curettage by holding the curette like a pen and scraping the endocervical canal in short, firm strokes.
If active bleeding is noted, apply Monsel's paste to the bleeding areas.
Withdraw the colposcope and gently remove the speculum.
side_effects_or_warnings: Serious cramping or lower abdominal pain
Pus-like discharge or fever (post-procedure infection signs)

step_by_step_guide: Patient should be unclothed from the waist up.
Inspect breasts for asymmetry, bulging, skin changes, or nipple retraction/discharge.
Begin with patient in seated position. Ask patient to raise arms over her head, then lower arms and place them on her hips, pressing to contract pectoralis muscles.
Palpate regional lymph nodes, paying special attention to axillary nodes.
Position patient supine with the arm on the side being examined raised above her head.
Palpate the entire chest wall using a radial approach, concentric circles, or vertical strips, using the pads of the fingers.
Swab sample onto a slide.
Heat fix by passing the slide through a flame.
Stain with crystal violet (60 seconds) and rinse.
Stain with iodine (60 seconds) and rinse.
Decolourise with acetone or ethanol for a few seconds (until the liquid runs clear).
Stain with safranin (60 seconds) and rinse.
Gently blot dry and examine under oil immersion (1000X). Gram-positive organisms appear purple, Gram-negative organisms appear red.

equipment_needed: ultrasound machine (with curved or phased array probe, and transvaginal probe)
condom or probe cover for transvaginal probe
step_by_step_guide: Trauma FAST: Place patient supine. Assess 4 regions for free fluid (black on screen).
Pericardial (subxiphoid): Aim probe into left chest, assess for free fluid between myocardium and pericardium.
Right upper quadrant (RUQ): Assess for free fluid between liver and kidney or superior to diaphragm.
Left upper quadrant (LUQ): Assess for free fluid between spleen and diaphragm, spleen and kidney.
Pelvic: View with full bladder. Assess for fluid between bladder and uterus/rectum.
Obstetric (1st trimester): Place patient supine. Transabdominal: scan suprapubic area with full bladder. Assess for intrauterine gestational sac, yolk sac, or fetal pole. Assess for free fluid.
If unable to view pregnancy transabdominally, empty bladder and use transvaginal probe with cover/gel. Scan entire uterus in sagittal and coronal views.
side_effects_or_warnings: Failure to find fluid in cases of haemoperitoneum
Misinterpreting pre-existing ascites or uroperitoneum as haemoperitoneum

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral morphine: 5 mg/5 ml or 50 mg/5 ml or tablets. Initially, 2.5–10 mg every 4 hours, increased by 30–50% if pain persists. IV (slow, 2 mg/min): 10 mg THEN 5–10 mg if necessary.
contraindications: raised intracranial pressure or head injury

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral: 0.5–1 g every 4–6 hours (max 4 g daily; max 2 g daily if hepatic impairment, cirrhosis). Rectally: 0.5–1 g every 4–6 hours.
contraindications: Do not exceed 4 grams in 24 hours (more can cause serious liver toxicity).

medical_records.adult_dosing: Acute coronary syndrome: Oral: 300 mg (preferably chewed or dispersed in water) given immediately as a single dose. Mild to moderate pain: 300 mg–600 mg every 4 hours as needed; maximum 4 grams in 24 hours.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to acetylsalicylic acid or any other NSAID
children and adolescents under 16 years (Reye syndrome)
side_effects_or_warnings: Gastrointestinal irritation with slight blood loss

medical_records.adult_dosing: 200–400 mg 3–4 times daily. Maximum 2.4 g daily.
contraindications: Avoid unless potential benefit greater than risk
Regular use in third trimester may cause closure of fetal ductus arteriosus

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral: 30 mg every 4 hours; maximum dose for pain 240 mg; consider switch to morphine when a dose of 180 mg is reached.
contraindications: Conditions where inhibition of peristalsis should be avoided
acute diarrhoeal conditions such as ulcerative colitis or antibiotic-associated colitis

medical_records.adult_dosing: SL: 5 mg sublingual, repeat in 10–15 minutes, not to exceed 10 mg every 2–3 hours.
IV (slow): 100 mg in a single dose. For Addison's syndrome/acute adrenal insufficiency: IV: 100 mg initially AND repeat every 8 hours. Convert to oral dose once patient stable.
contraindications: Systemic infection (unless life-threatening or specific antimicrobial therapy given)

medical_records.adult_dosing: IV: 8 mg; then repeat every 8 hours. Or IV/IM: Initially 24 mg daily; THEN reduce by 2 mg/day to lowest effective maintenance dose.
contraindications: Systemic infection (unless life-threatening or specific antimicrobial therapy given)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral: 40–60 mg. For PCP pneumonia: Oral: 40 mg twice daily for 5 days THEN 40 mg daily for 5 days THEN 20 mg daily for 11 days.
contraindications: Systemic infection (unless life-threatening or specific antimicrobial therapy given)

medical_records.adult_dosing: IV: Emergency dosing: Initially, 1–2 mmol/kg over 1–2 minutes. Additional dose: 0.5 mmol/kg every 10 minutes. Maintenance: 100–150 mmol sodium bicarbonate in 1 litre 5% dextrose at 250 ml/hour.
side_effects_or_warnings: Metabolic alkalosis (associated with muscle twitching, irritability, tetany)
pain, redness, or swelling at the injection site

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral: 20–50 mmol daily after meals. Slow IV infusion: 20–40 mmol/l in normal saline (not to exceed 10-20 mmol/hour).
IM/IV: 100 mg daily for 5 days THEN switch to oral 100 mg daily. For ethylene glycol poisoning: IM/IV: 100 mg every 8 hours for 6 doses.
IV (slow): Initially 15 mg/kg per hour THEN reduce after 4–6 hours so that total dose does not exceed 80 mg/kg in 24 hours.

medical_records.primary_topic: Ethanol (for Ethylene glycol or methanol poisoning)
medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral (or by NG): Loading: 1.8 ml/kg over 15–30 minutes (diluted). Maintenance dose: 0.2 ml/kg/hour (non-drinker) or 0.46 ml/kg/hour (heavy alcohol user).

For Hyperkalaemia: IV: 10–15 units in 50 ml D50 (50% dextrose water) infused over 2 hours THEN dextrose infusion + regular blood glucose monitoring. For DKA: IV bolus 0.15 U/kg followed by infusion at 0.1 U/kg/hour.
IM/IV: 250 mg/kg daily, divided every 4 hours (range 6–12 g/day). Or IV: 2 g every 4 hours.
contraindications: Severe hypersensitivity to penicillins and other beta-lactams

medical_records.adult_dosing: IV/IM (in anterior thigh): 20 mg/kg over 4 hours; THEN 10 mg/kg every 8 hours until oral medication is possible. Oral: 600 mg quinine sulfate 3 times daily for 7 days.
Oral: 500 mg 4 times daily. IV/oral: 500 mg 4 times daily.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to erythromycin or other macrolides

step_by_step_guide: Performed for circumferential, partial-, and full-thickness burns if swelling cuts off circulation.
Cut through the burned skin in the mid-lateral and mid-medial axes of the extremity.
Cut through the burn down to fat, allowing the skin to spread apart.
Put a little “T” at the end of the incision where burn meets normal skin to allow more expansion.
Never cut un-burned skin.
Cover with dressings.

clinical_signs: Signs of dehydration (decreased skin turgor, low urine output)
suspected_conditions: Excessive water loss (gastrointestinal, cutaneous, or renal)

clinical_signs: ECG: small or loss of P waves
ECG: widening of the QRS complex
suspected_conditions: Potassium-sparing diuretics or ACE inhibitors use

clinical_signs: Generalized weakness leading to ascending paralysis
ECG: flattened or absent T waves

suspected_conditions: Vitamin D or thiazide diuretics use

step_by_step_guide: Wet hands and apply soap.
Rub all surfaces, including wrists and spaces between the fingers, for at least 40-60 seconds.
Rinse hands thoroughly.
Dry thoroughly with a single use towel.
Use towel to turn off faucet and dispose of the used towel.

equipment_needed: Eye protection (eye visor, goggles) or face shield
Particulate respirator (e.g. N-95) if airborne precautions required
step_by_step_guide: To put on: 1. Assess risk. 2. Select and gather the necessary PPE. 3. Put on the gown. 4. Put on the mask. 5. Put on eye protection. 6. Put on gloves (over cuff).

To remove: 1. Peel off gown and gloves and roll inside-out. 2. Dispose of safely. 3. Perform hand hygiene. 4. Remove cap and eye protection (from behind head). 5. Put eye protection in a separate container for reprocessing. 6. Remove mask from behind head. 7. Perform hand hygiene.
side_effects_or_warnings: Neglecting to perform hand hygiene after removing PPE will reduce or negate any benefits of the protective equipment.
Avoid any contact between the soiled items and any area of the face.

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral: 500-1000 mg 3 times daily for 5-7 days (non-severe pneumonia). Oral: 1 gram 3 times daily for 7 days (sinusitis).
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity/anaphylaxis to penicillins or other beta-lactams
side_effects_or_warnings: Hypersensitivity reaction (discontinue if severe rash, urticaria, wheezing)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Vaginal candidiasis: Oral: 150 mg as a single dose. Oesophageal candidiasis: IV/oral: 100-200 mg daily for 14-21 days. Cryptococcal meningitis (secondary prophylaxis): Oral: 200 mg daily.

Primary prophylaxis for PLHIV: 1 DS tablet or 2 SS tablets once daily. Total daily dose is 960 mg (800 mg SMX + 160 mg TMP). PCP pneumonia: IV: 5 mg/kg (based on TMP component) 4 times daily for 21 days.
contraindications: Previous severe reactions to sulfa-containing drugs

medical_records.adult_dosing: Severe infections (e.g. acute pyelonephritis, typhoid fever): Oral: 500 mg twice daily OR IV: 400 mg twice daily for 10-14 days.
Oral: Rifampicin 10 mg/kg daily (max 300 mg) AND isoniazid 5 mg/kg (max 600 mg) AND pyrazinamide 25 mg/kg AND ethambutol 15 mg/kg once daily.
Depression / Neuropathic pain: Oral: Start 50 mg at bedtime AND increase gradually as necessary by 25-50 mg every 1-2 weeks to 100-150 mg daily.
contraindications: Patient has taken an MAO-I within 2 weeks

medical_records.adult_dosing: Strongyloidiasis: 200 mcg/kg as a single dose OR 200 mcg/kg daily for 2 days and then maintenance therapy 6 mg monthly. Scabies: 200 mcg/kg as a single dose and repeat in 2 weeks.
contraindications: Do not co-administer ivermectin if onchocerciasis or loaiasis are co-endemic with filariasis.
side_effects_or_warnings: Mazzotti reaction (fever, headache, cough, pruritus, conjunctivitis)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Streptococcal endocarditis: IV: 7.2-10.8 g (12-18 million IU) daily in 4 or 6 equally divided doses for 4 weeks. Severe anthrax / Leptospirosis: IV: 1.8-3.6 g every 4-6 hours.
contraindications: Severe hypersensitivity to penicillins or other beta-lactams

medical_records.primary_topic: Acute Fever (7 days or less) without clear focus
clinical_signs: Seriously ill with no obvious apparent cause
Constipation or diarrhoea, abdominal pain, rose spots
Pain behind the eyes, backache, arthralgia, petechiae
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Fever does not improve or comes back after treatment
Accompanied by cough, severe pain, confusion, stiff neck, or change in consciousness

medical_records.primary_topic: Prolonged Fever (More than 7 days) without clear focus
clinical_signs: Loss of weight, night sweats, malaise
Limb pain, local limb tenderness and swelling
Low grade fever, new or changing heart murmur, signs of embolic disease
Right upper quadrant pain or tenderness
Contact with farm animals, unpasteurized milk

medical_records.primary_topic: Bilateral Limb Swelling or Generalized Swelling
clinical_signs: Anasarca with periorbital oedema, heavy proteinuria
Jaundice, palmar erythema, spider angiomata, caput medusae, ascites
Wasted appearance, hair loss, hyperpigmentation, low serum albumin
Unilateral neck nodes, may be fluctuant or discharging, night sweats, weight loss
Painful, red, inflamed node with local source of infection
Tender inguinal lymphadenopathy, history of genital ulcers
Dark purple, painless lesions or nodules
Non-tender, enlarging nodes, systemic symptoms (fever, sweats, itch)
suspected_conditions: Sexually transmitted infection (e.g. chancroid, LGV, syphilis)

step_by_step_guide: Confirm the diagnosis of opioid dependence using history, physical examination, and urine analysis.
Initiate Methadone at a low dose (generally 20 mg, or 10 mg if tolerance is low) to avoid overdose.
Alternatively, initiate Buprenorphine at 4 mg only when objective signs of opioid withdrawal appear (at least 6-24 hours after last opioid use).
For Methadone, observe the patient daily in the first week. Maximum daily increase is 5-10 mg, with max dose of 40 mg by the end of week one.
Titrate to an optimal maintenance dose (60-120 mg for methadone, 12-24 mg for buprenorphine).
Provide psychosocial support, vocational training, and counseling during maintenance.
If reducing or ceasing OST, decrease gradually (e.g., methadone no faster than 5 mg/month) to avoid withdrawal and relapse.

medical_records.adult_dosing: 600 mg daily
side_effects_or_warnings: Hypersensitivity reaction (2-5% of patients) presenting with fever, fatigue, rash, sore throat, or shortness of breath
Never reintroduce Abacavir after discontinuation due to hypersensitivity, as re-exposure may lead to severe and potentially fatal anaphylactic reaction

medical_records.adult_dosing: Standard dose is 400/100 mg twice daily. Take with a meal.
5 mg/kg body weight daily (range 4-6 mg/kg); Maximum 300 mg daily. For intermittent 3 times per week dosing: 10 mg/kg (max 900 mg).
side_effects_or_warnings: Peripheral neuropathy (burning, numbness or tingling sensation in hands or feet)

medical_records.adult_dosing: 10 mg/kg body weight daily (range 8-12 mg/kg); Maximum 600 mg daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: Orange/red discoloration of urine and secretions (harmless)
Strong inducer of cytochrome P450; decreases efficacy of oral contraceptives and contraindicated with unadjusted boosted protease inhibitors

medical_records.adult_dosing: 25 mg/kg body weight daily (range 20-30 mg/kg).
15 mg/kg body weight daily (range 15-20 mg/kg).
side_effects_or_warnings: Require dose adjustment in renal failure

clinical_signs: Fluctuating level of consciousness within the day (often worsening at night)
Prominent difficulties in focusing, shifting, or maintaining attention
Difficulty orienting to place, time, and sometimes person
Evidence of underlying medical cause, infection, or drug/alcohol withdrawal
Distal, symmetrical glove and stocking distribution of sensory changes
Painful tingling, burning, or numbness starting in toes or soles
Reduced or absent ankle jerk reflexes
Impaired vibration sensation and temperature perception

step_by_step_guide: Evaluate and treat or remove the underlying cause (e.g., substitute offending drugs like d4T or ddI with AZT or TDF).
Give thiamine if there is a known history of alcohol use.
Give pyridoxine 50 to 75 mg daily if the neuropathy is related to HIV infection or INH use.
Initiate Amitriptyline for neuropathic pain control: start at 25-75 mg at night, titrate up to 300 mg daily as tolerated.
If amitriptyline is poorly tolerated or ineffective, use Carbamazepine (start 100 mg twice daily, increase up to 1600 mg daily).
In HIV-positive patients on ART with poor response, consider Gabapentin starting at 300 mg daily, titrating up to 3600 mg daily.

clinical_signs: Flat or raised fleshy growths anywhere in the anogenital area (HPV)
Flat, moist, malodorous, warty-like lesions (Condyloma lata)
Dome shaped papules with central depression (Molluscum contagiosum)
Non-healing penile lesion, indurated and ulcerating (Penile cancer)
Painful ulcers with ragged undermined borders and fluctuant bubo (Chancroid)
Painless progressive beefy red ulcer bleeding easily on contact (Donovanosis)

step_by_step_guide: Use syndromic management protocols for genital ulcer disease.
Administer first-line therapy: ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 3 days; OR erythromycin 500 mg orally 4 times daily for 7 days; OR azithromycin 1 gram orally as a single dose.
If first-line fails or as an alternative, give ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose.
Review the patient in 3-7 days to assess healing. If healing is poor, treatment should be repeated or prolonged (especially in HIV-positive patients).
Ensure partner notification and treatment.
Stage the disease using CSF examination. First stage: CSF leukocytes up to 5/ul without parasites. Second stage: Trypanosomes in CSF or leukocytes >5/ul.
For First stage T. b. rhodesiense: Administer Suramin IV.
For Second stage T. b. rhodesiense: Administer Melarsoprol IV (2.2 mg/kg/day for 10 days). Monitor for reactive encephalopathy in an intensive care setting.
For First stage T. b. gambiense: Administer Pentamidine deep IM (4 mg/kg/day for 7 days).
For Second stage T. b. gambiense: Administer combined treatment of oral Nifurtimox and IV Eflornithine.

clinical_signs: Itchy maculopapular rash after freshwater exposure (swimmers itch)
Acute fever, chills, cough, muscle aches, and eosinophilia (Katayama fever)
Chronic intestinal disease: abdominal pain, bloody diarrhoea, tender hepatomegaly
Chronic urogenital disease: supra-pubic pain, dysuria, terminal haematuria
Late stage: Portal hypertension, ascites, oesophageal varices, cor pulmonale

step_by_step_guide: Confirm diagnosis via urine dipstick (microscopic haematuria), direct microscopy of stool/urine for eggs, or serology.
Administer Praziquantel 40 mg/kg as a single dose.
Administer the dose between meals to minimize adverse events like abdominal pain, nausea, and headache.
Repeat the dose after 4 weeks if follow-up microscopy shows evidence of ongoing infection.
If central nervous system involvement is suspected, refer the patient to a tertiary level facility for further investigation and treatment.

medical_records.primary_topic: Breast Care and Infant Feeding for HIV-Positive Mothers
home_care_instructions: Exclusively breastfeed your infant for the first 6 months if breastfeeding is the chosen infant feeding method.
Ensure you take your ARV prophylaxis or ART daily as prescribed to protect your infant from HIV transmission.
