If replacement feeding is chosen, ensure you have safe water and sanitation at home, and exclusively give formula milk for the first 6 months.
Seek care promptly if you experience any breast problems, such as a breast abscess, mastitis, or nipple trauma, as these increase the risk of HIV transmission.
When you are ready to stop breastfeeding, transition gradually over about 1 month.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Redness, swelling, or extreme pain in the breasts
Infant develops diarrhoea or oral thrush

clinical_signs: Ill-defined diffuse swelling of the skin and subcutaneous tissues
Spreads to involve significant body surface area (Cellulitis)
Intense pain out of proportion to visible skin lesions, blistering, greyish necrotic tissue (Necrotizing fasciitis)

medical_records.primary_topic: Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections
step_by_step_guide: Assess for signs of severity or risk factors (severe disease, immunosuppression, very young/old age, lack of response to incision/drainage).
If abscess is present, incision and drainage is the mainstay of treatment.
In the absence of severe signs and risk factors, prescribe oral cloxacillin 500 mg 4 times daily for 7-10 days.
If the patient is allergic to penicillin or MRSA is highly suspected, use cotrimoxazole 1-2 DS tablets twice daily, doxycycline 100 mg twice daily, or clindamycin 300-450 mg 3 times daily.
Give parenteral therapy such as IV ceftriaxone or cloxacillin + clindamycin if systemic illness or severe signs are present. Switch to oral options after significant improvement.
If necrotizing fasciitis is suspected, urgent surgical intervention with debridement is mandatory.

clinical_signs: Shortness of breath mild to severe
Acute onset from hours to a few days
Focal chest pain with deep breaths or coughing
Focal signs such as bronchial breath sounds, crackles, or rales on auscultation
Dullness to percussion and decreased breath sounds if pleural effusion present

step_by_step_guide: Evaluate severity using clinical signs or CURB-65 criteria to determine the need for hospitalization.

For non-severe pneumonia managed as an outpatient, prescribe empirical antibiotics: amoxicillin 500-1000 mg 3 times daily, OR erythromycin 500 mg 4 times daily, OR doxycycline 100 mg 2 times daily for 5-7 days.
Advise the patient to return for evaluation if there is no improvement or a worsening of symptoms.
If not improving after 3 days and adherent to oral regimen, consider switching to an IV regimen (e.g. ceftriaxone 1-2 g once daily PLUS erythromycin).
Consider other infections, including TB and, if HIV-positive, consider PCP.
For severe pneumonia, admit to hospital, provide oxygen, and administer parenteral antibiotics immediately.

clinical_signs: Increased tone in muscles, often starting in the jaw (lockjaw)
Difficulty swallowing, stiffness and pain in the neck, shoulder, back, or abdominal muscles
Painful muscle spasms or arched back (opisthotonus)
Autonomic dysfunction: hypertension, tachycardia, high temperature, sweating
Alert mental state (normal level of consciousness)

step_by_step_guide: Administer antibiotics to reduce bacteria and stop toxin production: metronidazole 500 mg 4 times daily OR benzylpenicillin 2-4 million units every 6 hours for 10 days.
Give human tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) 500 units IM to bind circulating toxin.
Manage muscle spasms with diazepam titrated to control spasms (up to 500 mg per day), OR phenobarbital up to 1000-1500 mg, OR chlorpromazine.
For severe autonomic dysfunction, try labetalol, clonidine, or magnesium sulfate.
Provide intensive care with ventilatory support as respiratory failure can occur during severe spasms.
Administer tetanus toxoid vaccine for prevention and ensure appropriate wound care.

clinical_signs: Painless red eye with mucopurulent discharge and matting of eyelids (Bacterial conjunctivitis)
Watery, itchy, bilateral discharge with chemosis (Allergic conjunctivitis)
Painful red eye with sudden loss of vision, severe headache, halos around light, mid-dilated non-reactive pupil, and very high intraocular pressure (Acute angle-closure glaucoma)
Severe eye pain, decreased visual acuity, haziness or opacification of the cornea with fluorescein uptake, hypopyon (Corneal ulcer or keratitis)
Unilateral painful red eye, blurred vision, photophobia, ciliary injection (Acute anterior uveitis)

step_by_step_guide: Recognize the condition as an emergency to prevent irreversible visual loss and refer urgently to a specialist facility.
Start pilocarpine 1 drop (2% solution) every 10 minutes for 30-60 minutes.
Then administer 1 drop every 1-3 hours until intraocular pressure subsides, then 1 drop 4 times daily.
Administer acetazolamide 250 mg orally stat (or IV if the patient is vomiting).
Continue oral acetazolamide 500-750 mg per day until specialist evaluation.

clinical_signs: Nausea, anorexia, vomiting, fatigue, malaise, headaches, muscle aches
Clinical jaundice developing 1-2 weeks later (yellow skin and eyes, dark urine)
Enlarged, tender liver with right upper quadrant pain
AST and ALT elevated (at least 3 times higher than normal)
Low WBC at first, followed by an increase in lymphocytes

step_by_step_guide: For acute hepatitis A, B, or C, provide supportive care including fluid management.
Treat encephalopathy and coagulopathy if severe acute illness is present.
Avoid prescribing hepatotoxic drugs (e.g., specific ARVs or TB medications) during acute liver injury.
For chronic active hepatitis B (especially in HIV coinfection), initiate ART using tenofovir plus lamivudine (or emtricitabine) to avoid HBV resistance.
Monitor for hepatic flares, especially during the first weeks of treatment or if treatment is interrupted.
Provide hepatitis A and B vaccination for primary prevention in at-risk groups.

clinical_signs: Signs and symptoms of anaemia (pallor, shortness of breath on exertion, fatigue, dizziness)
Acute bone pain and joint swelling (vaso-occlusive crisis)
Haemoglobin usually 5-11 g/dl with sickle cells and target cells on blood film

step_by_step_guide: Prevent sickle crisis by avoiding precipitating factors including dehydration, hypoxaemia, infection, cold, and slowed circulation.
Give long-term prophylaxis with folic acid 1 mg daily orally.
Vaccinate with pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine and hepatitis B.
Recognize and treat malaria promptly.
For acute crisis: Rehydrate with oral fluids and IV normal saline if necessary.
Give oxygen if SpO2 is low.
Give effective pain relief using strong analgesics, including opiates (e.g. morphine).
Give blood transfusion for crises and severe acute anaemia (Hb <5 g/dl or >2 g/dl below baseline), aiming for a haemoglobin level of 7-8 g/dl only.

clinical_signs: Large joint involvement (knee most common, hip, shoulder, wrist, ankle)
Hot, tender, swollen joint with decreased mobility
Joint fluid WBC >50,000 (>90% neutrophils), purulent or turbid
History of diabetes, IV drug use, or sickle-cell disease
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Severe joint pain out of proportion to injury

step_by_step_guide: Provide adequate pain control.
Give IV antibiotics for 2-4 weeks, depending on clinical response and host factors.
Start ceftriaxone 1-2 g once daily.
If Gram-positive cocci are seen or S. aureus grows, switch to cloxacillin 2 g every 6 hours.
If MRSA is prevalent, administer vancomycin 15 mg/kg every 12 hours or cotrimoxazole.
Perform joint aspiration and washout to dryness to remove purulent fluid.

clinical_signs: Very inflamed throat with many exudates
Other features of common cold (runny nose, sneezing) are usually absent

step_by_step_guide: Assess clinical score (points for absence of cough, tender nodes, fever >38 C, tonsillar exudate, age <15) to justify empirical antibiotics.
Use rapid antigen detection test or culture if available to confirm.
Prescribe antibiotics to reduce the risk of rheumatic fever: benzathine benzylpenicillin G 1.2 million units IM single dose.
Alternatively, give phenoxymethyl penicillin 500 mg orally twice daily for 10 days.
If allergic to penicillin, give erythromycin 250 mg 4 times daily for 10 days.
Provide supportive management with fever reducing agents (e.g. paracetamol).

medical_records.primary_topic: Fever and suspected septic shock or malaria
immediate_actions: Give oxygen 5 litres if SpO2 <90 or respiratory distress.
Insert IV, give 1 litre bolus crystalloid (LR or NS) then reassess.
Give empirical antibiotics.
Give antimalarial and glucose (if blood glucose is low or unknown).
Send blood culture if feasible before starting antibiotics.

clinical_signs: Fever (temperature more than 38°C) or hypothermia (less than 36°C)
Warm extremities, bounding pulses (often not present) or weak, thready pulse and cold extremities when hypovolaemic from fluid shifts

Signs of infection: headache or neck stiffness (meningitis), severe rash, severe abdominal pain (peritonitis), cough or difficult breathing (pneumonia), painful urination or blood in the urine (pyelonephritis)

medical_records.primary_topic: Fever with altered level of consciousness
suspected_conditions: Sepsis from various causes including pneumonia, UTI

medical_records.primary_topic: Thin and thick blood films for malaria
step_by_step_guide: Place a small amount of blood near the middle of the slide for the thin film. Place two or three smaller drops off to the side for the thick film. Place the slide on a flat surface.
Hold another slide over the first at a 45 degree angle so that it just touches it. Slowly drag the upper (spreader) slide towards the drop of blood.
On contact with the spreader slide, the blood should spread along the width of the slide.
The spreader should then be drawn smoothly and rapidly in the opposite direction, producing a feathered edge.
Join the drops of blood intended for the thick film using a corner of the spreader slide. This should not require excessive stirring, only 3 to 6 circular or rectangular movements.
Allow the slide to air dry and label with a soft lead pencil.
Fix the thin film by adding a few drops of methanol and allow to dry. Try to avoid exposing the thick film to methanol.
Flood the slide with Giemsa solution and allow 30–45 minutes out of sunlight.
Rinse with water, drain, and air dry.
On the thick film, leukocyte nuclei should appear a deep, rich purple. Malaria parasites should have deep red chromatin and pale purplish blue cytoplasm.
Label slide carefully.
Using loop, take sputum sample from most dense portion of specimen (sample blood-specked, opaque, greyish, or yellowish cheesy mucus when present).
Smear the sample onto a slide over an area 2.0 X 1.0 cm; the broken end of a wooden stick may be used.
Air dry for 15 minutes.
Heat fix the sample by passing the slide smear side up through a Bunsen burner 3 times. The proper thickness of a heat fixed smear has been achieved when newsprint is just readable through it.
Flood the slide with carbol fuchsin.
Heat the slide until steam rises from the slide and wait 10 minutes.
Rinse with water and drain.
Flood the slide with decolouriser and wait 3 minutes.
Rinse with water and drain.
Flood the slide with methylene blue and wait 1 minute.
Rinse with water and drain.
Air dry.
Heat fix smear.
Acid-fast bacilli will appear as red, slender, rod-shaped bacilli against a blue background.

medical_records.adult_dosing: IM/IV: 2.4 mg/kg on admission AND repeat at 12 hours, 24 hours; then once daily. Start appropriate oral treatment as soon as tolerated, and give full course.
side_effects_or_warnings: ECG abnormalities (bradycardia, prolongation of the QT interval)
Post-treatment hemolysis has been observed with high cumulative doses

medical_records.adult_dosing: IV: 1 g daily for 7–14 days for severe infection. For meningitis: IV: 2 g twice daily for 5–14 days.
side_effects_or_warnings: pain and inflammation at injection site
blood disorders (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, agranulocytosis, aplastic anaemia, haemolytic anaemia)

medical_records.adult_dosing: IM/IV (slow): 4–6 mg/kg daily in divided doses every 8 hours for 2 weeks AND ampicillin. For initial empirical antibiotics in emergency management: IV: 240 mg in a single dose.
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to aminoglycoside group of antibiotics

medical_records.adult_dosing: IV: 1 gram every 6 hours. Epidemics of meningococcal meningitis: IM (oily): 100 mg/kg (maximum 3 g) as a single dose; repeat after 24–48 hours if necessary.
side_effects_or_warnings: grey baby syndrome (premature and newborn infants)

immediate_actions: Nothing by mouth (NPO)
IV fluids
Give oxygen if respiratory distress or SpO2 <90
Empirical antibiotics IV/IM
Treat pain
Suspect surgical abdomen - call for help
send blood for type and cross match
If early pregnancy possible, consider ectopic and check rapid pregnancy test

clinical_signs: Bleeding: GI bleeding (vomiting or in stools)
SBP <90 mmHg or pulse >110 per minute
immediate_actions: Call for help
Insert IV and give fluids rapidly
Send blood specimen for type and cross match then transfuse as needed
Repeat Quick Check and monitor pulse, SBP and haemoglobin
Insert nasogastric tube to decompress - do not lavage
If endoscope and trained provider: locate site and cauterize
Give proton pump inhibitor in high dose (e.g. omeprazole 80 mg)
Check whole blood clotting time

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
drainage bag and tubing or IV drip giving set
step_by_step_guide: The patient should be instructed to empty their bladder. Occasionally, insertion of a urinary catheter may be required.
Patients with significant ascites can be positioned lying face up; those with less ascites can be positioned lying down on the left side.

The left lower quadrant (2-3 cm lateral to the border of the rectus muscles) has been shown to be a good site for paracentesis. The right lower quadrant and a site 3-4 cm below the umbilicus have also been used.
Cleanse the area with antiseptic.
Anaesthetize the puncture site with lidocaine.
Carefully insert the needle at the site. A small amount of give may be felt as the needle enters the peritoneal cavity. Caution is required to avoid sudden penetration of the needle.

Remove only the necessary amount of fluid. A drainage bag attached to the needle with tubing may be used when large amounts of fluid must be removed. Note that removal of more than 1 litre of fluid may result in post-paracentesis hypotension.
contraindications: a bleeding diathesis (other than DIC) as the risk of bleeding is very low
infection or surgical scars at the site of needle entry

step_by_step_guide: Elevate the head of the bed, or ask the patient to assume an upright, sitting position.

In order to determine the appropriate length of tubing to be inserted, measure from the xyphoid (bottom of the sternum or breastbone) to the ear and then to the nose. Add 15 cm to this distance to obtain the insertion distance. The NG tube itself may be used to measure, marking the approximate point on the tube with tape.
Lubricate the tube with a liberal quantity of water-based lubricant prior to insertion.

The tube should then be inserted gently in the posterior (not superior) direction. Proceed gently to avoid trauma to the tissue behind the nose. If there is resistance, attempt to use the other nostril.
If the patient is having difficulty, instruct them to sip some water while simultaneously trying to pass the tube.
The patient can help direct the tube into the oesophagus by putting their chin to their chest. Tracheal insertion should be suspected if there is excessive coughing or condensation inside the tube.

Make sure to confirm placement of the tube before using it, especially in patients with an altered level of consciousness. Successful placement in the stomach can be confirmed by rapidly pushing air into the tube with a large syringe; there should be gurgling sounds which can be heard through a stethoscope placed on the stomach. A chest X-ray may be done to confirm placement.

The tube should be secured carefully to the nose and the patient's gown (to avoid displacing the tube if there is a sudden tug). A butterfly type bandage or tape may be used to secure the tube to the nose. Avoid the tube pressing on the medial or lateral aspects of the inner nostril, as this may result in necrosis or bleeding.
contraindications: recent alkali ingestion (may cause oesophageal perforation)
side_effects_or_warnings: Gastric erosions and bleeding if the tube is in place long term

step_by_step_guide: Patients who are comatose or unable to protect the airway must be intubated prior to lavage. If intubation is not possible, lavage should not be attempted.
Place the patient on their left side with the head down by 15-30 degrees. This is important to reduce the risk of aspiration.
Measure and mark the length of tube needed before insertion.

If the patient has ingested a solid poison (e.g. tablets), insert an appropriately sized (French 36-40) and properly lubricated orogastric tube. If the patient vomits, carefully and quickly apply suction to remove the vomitus. Do not use force to pass the tube.

If the patient has ingested a liquid poison (e.g. pesticide), insert a properly lubricated nasogastric tube. If the patient vomits, carefully and quickly apply suction to remove the vomitus. Do not use force to pass the tube.
Check the proper positioning of the tube in the stomach by air insufflation or aspiration with pH testing of aspirate.
Instil and lavage with no more than 100-300 ml lukewarm or tepid water or normal saline. Remove the fluid before giving more. Repeat until 1-2 litres have been given and removed.
contraindications: unconsciousness or depressed sensorium with unprotected airway (possibility of aspiration)
ingestion of corrosive substances because of the danger of perforation
ingestion of hydrocarbons, unless a more toxic substance is combined with the hydrocarbon, such as pesticide (possibility of aspiration)
presence of frank convulsions (possibility of aspiration)
patient at risk of haemorrhage or gastrointestinal perforation
an uncooperative patient (the tube can injure the gastrointestinal tract)
side_effects_or_warnings: mechanical injury to the throat, oesophagus and stomach

medical_records.adult_dosing: Gastritis/peptic ulcer disease (PUD): Oral: 20 mg daily for 4-8 weeks. Helicobacter pylori gastritis: Oral: 20 mg twice daily AND clarithromycin + amoxicillin.
side_effects_or_warnings: increased risk of gastro-intestinal infections (including C. difficile infection)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral/IV: 10 mg every 8 hours. 15-19 years (<60 kg): 5 mg 3 times daily.
contraindications: Gastrointestinal obstruction (often used to empty the stomach of blood prior to EGD)
side_effects_or_warnings: cardiac conduction abnormalities following IV administration

medical_records.adult_dosing: 20-30 g (30-45 ml) 3-4 times daily; adjust dose every 1-2 days to produce 2-3 soft stools daily.
Oral: 2-4 tablets, usually at night. Initial dose should be low; then gradually increased to 30 mg.

step_by_step_guide: Determine ART eligibility based on CD4 count and WHO clinical stage.
Start ART in all patients with CD4 count 350 cells/mm3 or below, irrespective of clinical stage.
Start ART in all patients in WHO clinical stage 3 or 4, irrespective of CD4 count.
Start ART in all patients with HIV/TB coinfection or HIV/HBV coinfection with chronic active hepatitis B, irrespective of CD4 count.
Determine the appropriate first-line ARV regimen (typically 2 NRTIs plus 1 NNRTI). Preferred regimens include AZT or TDF + 3TC or FTC + EFV or NVP.
Prepare the patient for adherence to the lifelong treatment.
Monitor the response to ART clinically, immunologically (CD4 count), and virologically (viral load) if available.
Monitor carefully for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) and drug toxicities.

medical_records.adult_dosing: 300 mg twice daily
side_effects_or_warnings: Bone marrow suppression resulting in rapid onset of life-threatening anaemia or neutropaenia

medical_records.adult_dosing: 600 mg once daily
side_effects_or_warnings: CNS symptoms in the first 2-4 weeks such as drowsiness, dizziness, vivid dreams, or persistent hallucinations
Potential teratogenicity if taken during the first trimester of pregnancy

clinical_signs: Low bicarbonate and high anion gap (>13)
suspected_conditions: Lactic acidosis due to NRTI toxicity (e.g. d4T, ddI, or AZT)

step_by_step_guide: Perform a lumbar puncture and CSF examination; diagnose via India ink stain or Cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) test.
Monitor and treat raised intracranial pressure with frequent (daily) lumbar punctures to relieve pressure and avoid blindness.
Administer Option 1 (if available): amphotericin B 0.7 to 1 mg/kg PLUS flucytosine 100 mg/kg for 14 days, followed by oral fluconazole 400 mg daily for 8 weeks.
If flucytosine is unavailable, use Option 2: amphotericin B PLUS oral fluconazole 800 mg for 14 days, followed by fluconazole 400 mg for 8 weeks.
Ensure pre-hydration and electrolyte monitoring when using amphotericin B to prevent life-threatening hypokalaemia and renal impairment.
Defer ART initiation until 2-4 weeks of induction and consolidation treatment have been completed to minimize risk of CNS IRIS.
Provide secondary prophylaxis with fluconazole 200 mg daily until the patient is successfully on ART and the CD4 count is maintained above 200 cells/mm3 for at least 6 months.

clinical_signs: Pain in the chest behind the sternum (retrosternal pain)
suspected_conditions: Kaposi sarcoma in the oral cavity

step_by_step_guide: Initiate empirical treatment with fluconazole tablet 100 mg or 200 mg daily for 14 to 21 days.
Alternatively, prescribe itraconazole 200 mg daily (up to 400 mg) for 10-14 days.
If neither fluconazole nor itraconazole is available, use ketoconazole 200 to 400 mg daily until remission (avoid co-administration with nevirapine).
If symptoms do not improve after 7 days, consider treating for CMV, HSV, or reflux disease.
Initiate ART as soon as the patient can swallow comfortably, as oesophageal candidiasis is a WHO clinical stage 4 condition.
Provide a soft diet and avoid extremely hot, cold, or spicy foods.

clinical_signs: Macular and papular purple lesions or nodular tumours on skin
Dark, patchy, painless swellings or non-pruritic nodules
Lesions located mainly on lower limbs, face, oral mucosa, and genitalia
Lymphoedema of the face, lower limbs, or genitalia
Lesions tend to follow lines of skin cleavage

step_by_step_guide: Recommend HIV testing and counselling to all pregnant women at their first antenatal care visit.
Assess WHO clinical stage and CD4 count to determine ART eligibility for the mother's own health.
Start lifelong ART if CD4 is 350 or below, or if the woman is in WHO stage 3 or 4.
If not eligible for ART, initiate Maternal triple ARV prophylaxis (Option B) or Maternal AZT prophylaxis (Option A) starting as early as 14 weeks gestation.
Continue Maternal triple ARV prophylaxis until delivery, or if breastfeeding, continue until 1 week after all infant exposure to breast milk has ended.
For Option A, give AZT 300 mg twice daily during pregnancy, single-dose NVP at the onset of labour plus AZT and 3TC twice daily for 7 days postpartum.
Ensure the infant receives the recommended daily NVP or AZT prophylaxis for 4-6 weeks after birth.
Provide cotrimoxazole prophylaxis for all HIV-positive TB patients throughout the course of anti-TB treatment.
Start TB treatment first.
Initiate ART in all HIV-positive individuals with active TB irrespective of their CD4 cell count.
Start ART as soon as clinically possible after starting TB treatment, usually within 8 weeks.
Use an Efavirenz (EFV)-based ART regimen (e.g. TDF + 3TC + EFV) for patients starting ART while on TB treatment, as the interaction with rifampicin is easier to manage.
Monitor for TB immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (TB-IRIS), which typically presents within 3 months of ART initiation as worsening of symptoms.
Provide pyridoxine 10 mg daily to prevent peripheral neuropathy in all HIV-positive patients receiving an anti-TB drug regimen containing isoniazid.
Ensure the patient does not have a history of a grade 4 toxicity (e.g., Stevens-Johnson syndrome or toxic epidermal necrolysis) to cotrimoxazole.
Wait 2 weeks after a non-severe (grade 3 or less) cotrimoxazole toxicity has resolved before attempting desensitization.
Initiate an antihistamine drug the day before desensitization, and continue daily until completing the dose escalation.
Day 1: Give 80 mg sulfamethoxazole + 16 mg trimethoprim (2 ml of oral suspension).
Day 2: Give 160 mg sulfamethoxazole + 32 mg trimethoprim (4 ml of oral suspension).
Day 3: Give 240 mg sulfamethoxazole + 48 mg trimethoprim (6 ml of oral suspension).
Day 4: Give 320 mg sulfamethoxazole + 64 mg trimethoprim (8 ml of oral suspension).
Day 5: Give 1 single-strength tablet (400 mg sulfamethoxazole + 80 mg trimethoprim).
Day 6 onward: Give 2 single-strength tablets or 1 double-strength tablet (800 mg sulfamethoxazole + 160 mg trimethoprim) daily.
If a severe reaction occurs during escalation, stop the desensitization.

medical_records.adult_dosing: Lead-in dose of 200 mg daily for the first 2 weeks, then escalate to full dose of 200 mg twice daily.

clinical_signs: Subacute onset over days to weeks
Dull affect, altered level of consciousness
Focal neurological signs (cranial nerve deficits, motor deficits, visual field loss, aphasia)
CD4 count typically less than 100 cells/mm3

step_by_step_guide: Initiate Option 1 (first choice in resource-limited settings): cotrimoxazole 2 double-strength tablets 3 times daily for 6 weeks.
If unable to take oral medication and severely ill, use IV cotrimoxazole.
Option 2: Pyrimethamine 100-200 mg loading dose once, then 50 mg once daily for 6 weeks, PLUS sulfadiazine 4 to 6 grams 4 times daily for 6 weeks, PLUS folinic acid 10-25 mg daily.
If intracranial pressure is elevated, administer prednisolone (40 mg 4 times daily) or dexamethasone (4 mg 4 times daily) to manage cerebral oedema if TB is excluded.
Expect clinical response after 7 to 14 days; if no improvement occurs, reconsider the diagnosis.
Provide secondary prophylaxis with cotrimoxazole 1 double-strength tablet daily until CD4 is greater than 200 for at least 6 months on ART.

clinical_signs: Homogenous, thin white or greyish discharge with fishy odour (Bacterial Vaginosis)
Purulent or mucopurulent discharge with cervical friability (Gonorrhoea/Chlamydia)

step_by_step_guide: Screen all persons living with HIV, including pregnant women, for active TB at each facility visit by asking about current cough, fever, weight loss, or night sweats.
If the patient has no symptoms of active TB, offer IPT as part of a comprehensive package of HIV care.
Prescribe Isoniazid 300 mg daily for at least 6 months, and up to 36 months in settings with high TB prevalence.
Administer pyridoxine 10 mg daily concurrently to prevent peripheral neuropathy.
Defer IPT in the presence of active hepatitis (acute or chronic), regular and heavy alcohol consumption, and symptoms of peripheral neuropathy.
Monitor for signs of hepatitis (e.g., jaundice, anorexia, nausea) and instruct the patient to discontinue INH and seek care if these occur.

medical_records.adult_dosing: 300 mg daily

step_by_step_guide: Assess for co-existing medical conditions and provide appropriate treatment.
For initial treatment (stabilization), if the patient is able to consume food, give ready-to-use therapeutic foods (e.g., F75, F100) or fortified blended flours.
Do not combine therapeutic foods with additional vitamins and minerals, as they are already fortified to correct deficiencies.
Aim initially to prevent further tissue loss, using calculated dietary requirements based on age and weight (e.g., 40 Kcal/kg for ages 19-75).
Treat and prevent hypothermia, hypoglycaemia, and dehydration.
Do not stop antiretroviral medication during re-feeding in patients with HIV; if not yet on ART, defer initiation until the rehabilitation phase.
During rehabilitation, provide a diet based on traditional foods with added oil, mineral mix, and vitamin mix.
Discharge when the patient is eating well, gaining weight, and has a reliable source of nutritious food outside the hospital.

clinical_signs: Sudden onset of fever lasting 2 to 7 days
Warning signs: abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleed, clinical fluid accumulation, lethargy, liver enlargement >2cm
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Severe plasma leakage with shock or fluid accumulation with respiratory distress
Severe organ impairment (AST or ALT >1000, impaired consciousness)

step_by_step_guide: For patients without warning signs (Group A): send home, advise adequate bed rest, adequate fluid intake, and paracetamol (maximum 4 g daily in adults).
For patients with warning signs (Group B): refer for inpatient hospital care, encourage oral fluids, or start IV fluid therapy with Normal Saline (NS) or Lactated Ringer's (LR) at maintenance rate.
For severe dengue (Group C): start IV fluid resuscitation with NS or LR at 5-10 ml/kg/hour over 1 hour.
Reassess the patient's condition and haematocrit; if improving, reduce IV fluids gradually.
If hypotensive shock is present, initiate IV fluid resuscitation with NS or LR or colloid solution at 20 ml/kg as a bolus for 15 minutes.
Monitor for haemorrhagic complications and transfuse fresh packed red cells or fresh whole blood if severe bleeding is identified.
recommended_tests: Rapid diagnostic test for NS1 antigen (day 1 to 6 of illness)
Detection of IgM/IgG by RDT or ELISA (day 5 onwards)

step_by_step_guide: Treat as a medical emergency; assess airway, breathing, and circulation.
Check blood glucose; if less than 2.2 mmol/l (or 3 mmol/l in pregnant/comatose patients), give IV glucose immediately.
Treat convulsions with intravenous or rectal diazepam or intramuscular paraldehyde.
Give intravenous artesunate 2.4 mg/kg body weight on admission (time=0), then at 12 hours and 24 hours, then once daily.
If parenteral artesunate is unavailable, give rate-controlled IV infusion of quinine 20 mg salt/kg on admission, then 10 mg/kg every 8 hours.
As soon as the patient can tolerate oral medication, complete the treatment with a full course of an Artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT).

clinical_signs: Severe dehydration (sunken eyes, lethargy, poor skin pinch)
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Skin pinch goes back very slowly

step_by_step_guide: Classify dehydration severity (severe, some, or none).
Rehydration is the mainstay of treatment; use ORS for 80% of cases or IV Ringer's lactate for severe dehydration.
Provide antibiotics for patients with severe disease (e.g., single dose doxycycline 300 mg, or tetracycline 500 mg 4 times daily for 3 days, or ciprofloxacin 1 g single dose).
Isolate patients in a separate ward.
Ensure strict hand hygiene and safe water supply to prevent transmission.

clinical_signs: History of wild animal or unimmunized domestic animal bite (especially dogs, cats, bats)
Paresthesias (pins and needles) around the bite area
Hydrophobia (fear of water) and aerophobia (fear of air)

step_by_step_guide: Immediately scrub the wound with alkaline soap and water, and flush with water for 15 minutes.
Apply povidone-iodine or benzalkonium chloride 1-4% to the wound.
Categorize the contact: Category I (touching/feeding), Category II (nibbling, minor scratches without bleeding), Category III (transdermal bites, licks on broken skin, bat exposure).
For Category I, no treatment is required.
For Category II, initiate immediate vaccination using tissue-culture or purified duck-embryo vaccines.
For Category III, administer immediate vaccination PLUS rabies immunoglobulin.
Inject human rabies immune globulin 20 IU/kg or equine rabies immunoglobulin 40 IU/kg mostly at the site of the bite.
Administer vaccine using standard intramuscular schedule (days 0, 3, 7, 14, and 30) in the deltoid, or an approved intradermal schedule.

clinical_signs: Productive cough lasting more than 2 weeks
Chest pain or shortness of breath
Enlarged, painless, matted lymph nodes (in extrapulmonary TB)

step_by_step_guide: Diagnose using sputum microscopy for AFB or Xpert MTB/RIF test; consider chest X-ray and clinical algorithms for smear-negative cases.
Initiate a standardized TB treatment regimen based on national guidelines (e.g., 2 months of HRZE intensive phase, followed by 4 months of HR continuation phase).
Provide daily treatment with fixed-dose combinations (FDCs) where possible.
In HIV-positive patients, start TB treatment first, followed by ART as soon as possible (usually within 8 weeks).
Monitor clinical response, patient's weight, and sputum smear microscopy after 2 months.
Manage minor side effects symptomatically and major side effects (like jaundice, deafness, shock) by stopping the responsible drugs.
For suspected MDR-TB, obtain culture and drug-susceptibility testing (DST) and refer to national guidelines for second-line regimens.

clinical_signs: Irregular and prolonged fever (more than 2 weeks)
Primary: Single, painless, indurated, clean ulcer (chancre) on genitals, anal canal, or mouth
Secondary: Papulosquamous rash involving palms and soles
Secondary: Mucous patches and condylomata lata
Secondary: Fever, malaise, sore throat, generalized lymphadenopathy
Tertiary: Cutaneous gummas, aortitis, neurological deficits (general paresis, tabes dorsalis)

step_by_step_guide: Confirm diagnosis with non-treponemal tests (RPR, VDRL) and treponemal confirmatory tests.
For primary, secondary, and early latent syphilis (<2 years), give a single dose of benzathine benzylpenicillin G, 2.4 million U IM (12 ml total, 6 ml in each buttock).
For non-pregnant, penicillin-allergic patients, give doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily or tetracycline 500 mg orally 4 times daily for 14 days.
For late latent syphilis (>2 years) or undetermined duration, give benzathine benzylpenicillin G, 2.4 million U IM once weekly for 3 consecutive weeks.
For neurosyphilis, give aqueous benzyl penicillin G 2-4 million U IV every 4 hours for 14 days.
Monitor VDRL/RPR titres; they should decline fourfold over the 6-12 months after treatment.
Ensure partner notification and treatment.

medical_records.primary_topic: Vaginal bleeding in pregnant woman or woman of childbearing age
clinical_signs: Heavy bleeding: pad or cloth soaked in <5 minutes
Any bleeding in late pregnancy (uterus above umbilicus)
Bleeding more than 100 ml since labour began
immediate_actions: Insert an IV line
Give fluids rapidly
If early pregnancy, give 0.2 mg ergometrine IM (repeat if continues)
If late pregnancy, DO NOT do vaginal examination
Call for help and admit to hospital
Consider ectopic pregnancy if light bleeding and abdominal pain

clinical_signs: Heavy bleeding: pad or cloth soaked in <5 minutes
Bleeding >250 ml or delivered outside hospital and still bleeding
Uterus remains soft after placenta delivered
immediate_actions: Call for extra help
Massage uterus until it is hard
Give oxytocin 10 IU IM
Insert IV line and give IV fluids with 20 IU oxytocin/litre at 60 drops/minute
Empty bladder, catheterize if necessary
Check and record SBP and pulse every 15 minutes and treat
If bleeding continues and uterus hard, check for perineal and lower vaginal tears
If bleeding continues and placenta not delivered, remove placenta manually

clinical_signs: Convulsing in second half of pregnancy or post-partum up to one week
immediate_actions: Protect from fall or injury
Manage airway and assist into recovery position
Give oxygen 5 litres
Give magnesium sulfate if diastolic >110 mmHg with proteinuria or if convulsing
Do not leave the woman on her own
Give IV fluids slowly
Consider caesarian section unless delivery is imminent

step_by_step_guide: Explain to the woman the need for manual removal of the placenta and obtain her consent.
Insert an IV line. If bleeding, give fluids rapidly. If not bleeding, give fluids slowly.
Assist woman to get onto her back.
Give diazepam (10 mg IV) or ketamine sedation if not comatose.
Clean vulva and perineal area.
Ensure the bladder is empty. Catheterize if necessary.
Wash hands and forearms well and put on long sterile gloves.
With the left hand, hold the umbilical cord with the clamp. Then pull the cord gently until it is horizontal.
Insert right hand into the vagina and up into the uterus.
Leave the cord and hold the fundus with the left hand in order to support the fundus of the uterus and to provide counter-traction during removal.
Move the fingers of the right hand sideways until edge of the placenta is located.
Detach the placenta from the implantation site by keeping the fingers tightly together and using the edge of the hand to gradually make a space between the placenta and the uterine wall.
Withdraw the right hand from the uterus gradually, bringing the placenta with it.
Explore the inside of the uterine cavity to ensure all placental tissue has been removed.
With the left hand, provide counter-traction to the fundus through the abdomen by pushing it in the opposite direction of the hand that is being withdrawn (prevents inversion of the uterus).
Examine the uterine surface of the placenta to ensure that lobes and membranes are complete.
contraindications: Placenta is retained due to constriction ring or closed cervix (do not persist)
Placenta does not separate from the uterine surface by gentle sideways movement (suspect placenta accreta, do not persist)

step_by_step_guide: Bimanual compression: Wear sterile or clean gloves.
Introduce the right hand into the vagina, clenched fist, with the back of the hand directed posteriorly and the knuckles in the anterior fornix.
Place the other hand on the abdomen behind the uterus and squeeze the uterus firmly between the two hands.
Continue compression until bleeding stops.
Aortic compression (if bimanual fails): Feel for femoral pulse.
Apply pressure above the umbilicus to stop bleeding. Apply sufficient pressure until femoral pulse is not felt.
Continue pressure until bleeding stops or while preparing for surgery/transport.

medical_records.primary_topic: Condom over foley catheter to tamponade uterine bleeding
step_by_step_guide: Insert sterile foley catheter up to 3–5 cm below the bulb into a condom.
Tie the condom tightly around the stem of the catheter using sterile gauze ties.
Using a sterile speculum and sponge holding forceps, insert catheter with the condom attached well into the uterine cavity.
Clamp the catheter and leave the end inside the vagina.
Connect a bag of sterile fluid to the end of the catheter (ensuring a tight fit) and allow the fluid to run in and fill the catheter.
Make arrangements for further treatment as appropriate.
Deflate the foley catheter after 24 hours, and if bleeding does not recur remove it.

medical_records.adult_dosing: Severe pre-eclampsia and eclampsia: Give IV and IM combined dose (loading dose): Insert IV line and give fluids slowly. Give 4 g of magnesium sulfate (20 ml of 20% solution) IV slowly over 20 minutes AND Give 10 g of magnesium sulfate IM: give 5 g (10 ml of 50% solution) IM deep in upper outer quadrant of each buttock with 1 ml of 2% lidocaine. If convulsions recur after 15 minutes, give an additional 2 g of magnesium sulfate IV over 20 minutes.

Treatment of postpartum and post-abortion haemorrhage: IM 10 IU AND start IV fluids with 20 IU oxytocin at 60 drops/minute. Continue oxytocin at 20 IU at 20 drops/minute for at least 1 hour after bleeding stops.
contraindications: any condition where spontaneous labour or vaginal delivery inadvisable
Avoid prolonged administration in oxytocin-resistant uterine inertia, severe pre-eclamptic toxaemia, severe cardiovascular disease.
side_effects_or_warnings: uterine hyperstimulation (may cause fetal distress, asphyxia, tetanic contractions, soft-tissue damage or uterine rupture)
water intoxication and hyponatraemia associated with high doses

medical_records.adult_dosing: Postpartum haemorrhage not responding to oxytocin plus ergometrine: SL: 800 mcg. Incomplete abortion: Oral: Single dose of 400 mcg sublingually or 600 mcg by mouth.
contraindications: See other sources for contraindications to use for induction of labour (such as placenta praevia, cephalopelvic distortion, history caesarean section or major uterine surgery, etc.)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Haemorrhage in early pregnancy: IM: 0.2 mg in a single dose AND repeat IM or IV if bleeding continues. Postpartum haemorrhage: IV (slow) or IM: 0.2 mg.
contraindications: Contraindicated at induction of labour or first and second stages of labour

medical_records.adult_dosing: 5 mg/kg/day, divided in 2 or 3 daily doses (PO) over 60 consecutive days. Preferably after meals. Maximum daily dose 300 mg.
10 mg/kg/day divided in 2 or 3 daily doses (PO) over 60 consecutive days. Preferably after meals.
side_effects_or_warnings: Nauseas, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, anorexia
Central nervous system alterations (sleep disturbances, excitatory states, seizures, psychotic behaviour)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 1.5-3 mg once daily. Can be titrated up to 20 mg daily. Typical effective dose: 3-20 mg daily. Use lowest effective dose. (Elderly or medically ill: Initiate 0.5-1 mg once daily).
side_effects_or_warnings: Acute dystonic reaction or severe muscle spasm
Increased risk of death in elderly patients with dementia

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 75 mg at night. Can be titrated up to 300 mg daily. Typical effective dose is 75-300 mg daily, up to 1000 mg may be necessary in severe cases.
side_effects_or_warnings: Severe hypotension resulting in falls (especially in elderly)
Acute dystonic reaction or severe muscle spasm

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 500 mg at night. Increase the dose by 200 mg every 7 days. Typical effective dose is 1000-2000 mg daily. (Elderly: Initiate 200 mg in the morning and 200 mg at night).
Initiate treatment with 200 mg at night. Increase the dose by 200 mg every 3-4 days until there is a clinical response. Typical effective dose is 400-600 mg daily, max 1000 mg.
side_effects_or_warnings: Severe hypersensitivity or allergic reactions of skin or organs
Hepatitis or hepatic failure or pancreatitis

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 2 mg 1 to 3 times daily. May increase gradually if needed to 5-10 mg twice daily. Maximum dose is 20 mg during a 24-hour period.
side_effects_or_warnings: Long-term treatment can cause substance dependence
Sudden discontinuation can cause withdrawal syndrome

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initially, morphine sulphate 2.5-10 mg every 4 hours, increased by 30-50% if pain persists. There is NO ceiling dose.
Initial dose is generally 20 mg (10 mg if tolerance is low). Maximum daily increase is 5-10 mg. Maximum daily dose at end of first week 40 mg. Optimal maintenance dose is 60-120 mg.
side_effects_or_warnings: Risk of overdose and death during induction
Potential for weight gain and dental problems

medical_records.adult_dosing: Starting dose is 4 mg sublingually. Increase to 8 mg the following day. Optimal dose is 12-24 mg daily, maximum 32 mg daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: Can precipitate withdrawal if given too soon after full agonist
Can lead to death if combined with other sedatives

medical_records.adult_dosing: 2.4 mg/kg body weight IV or IM on admission (time=0), then at 12 hours and 24 hours, then once daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: Solution should be prepared freshly for each administration and should not be stored
Requires specific reconstitution with 5% sodium bicarbonate and dilution with 5% dextrose or normal saline

medical_records.adult_dosing: 20 mg salt/kg body weight on admission (IV infusion or divided IM injection), then 10 mg/kg body weight every 8 hours. Rate-controlled IV infusion not exceeding 5 mg salt/kg body weight per hour.
side_effects_or_warnings: Lethal hypotension if given by rapid IV bolus
Undiluted solution is highly acidic and painful if given IM

clinical_signs: Characteristics of any attempt, past or present
Presence of a severe mental disorder
Presence of substance intoxication or disorder
Psychiatric disorders (generally depression, alcoholism, and personality disorders)
Physical illness (terminal, painful or debilitating illness, AIDS)
Family history of suicide, alcoholism, or other psychiatric disorders
Signs of emotional distress, hopelessness, agitation, uncommunicative behaviour, social isolation
suspected_conditions: Imminent risk of self-harm or suicide

step_by_step_guide: If a suicide attempt has been made, observe for signs of self-injury and urgently treat associated medical complications.
Remove means of self-harm.

If a suicide attempt has been made or a plan or threat exists for imminent suicide: do not leave patient alone; talk gently with the patient; keep the patient in a secure and supportive environment in the facility.
Assign a named staff member or family member for continued monitoring to ensure patient safety.
Assess for underlying mental disorders and start appropriate treatment.
In situations where there is imminent risk of serious self-harm, urgent referral to a mental health service is recommended. If not available, activate psychosocial support.
Assess for and begin treatment of co-morbid problems: physical (including chronic pain, epilepsy), mental disorders and substance use.
Work to understand and lessen suicidal feelings; explore reasons to stay alive.
Provide information about community-based services and patient support or self-help groups and facilitate access to these services.

The individual, family, and relevant others should be advised to restrict access to the means for self-harm (e.g. pesticides and other toxic substances, medication, firearms) as long as the individual has thoughts or plans of self-harm.

clinical_signs: Agitation, frightening or unusually impulsive behaviour
Unkempt appearance and odd ways of relating to others, or odd mannerisms
Disorganized or strange speech, thoughts, or behaviour
Reporting or responding to hallucinations (e.g. reacting to false or imagined perceptions, such as talking to imaginary people)
Reporting or responding to delusions (e.g. fixed false beliefs)
Severe self-neglect; inability or disinterest in caring for self
Inappropriate or narrowed range of emotions
Impairment in cognition, memory, and attention
suspected_conditions: Delirium due to alcohol or sedative withdrawal
Intoxication from alcohol or other substance
Dementia due to HIV infection (HIV-associated dementia)

step_by_step_guide: Assessment for the safety of the patient and others should be performed as soon as possible.
Initial hospitalization often is required for acute stabilization, especially for patients who are at imminent risk of violence or self-harm.
Assessment for an underlying medical etiology and the initiation of appropriate medical treatment are essential.
Provide psycho-education that supports the patient's recovery. Instil realistic hope and emphasize the importance of continuing regular activities.
Support the family and provide guidance in their interactions with the patient.
Antipsychotic medication is the mainstay of the acute management of psychotic symptoms. Haloperidol or chlorpromazine should be routinely considered.
The minimal effective dose of antipsychotics should be used, paying attention to minimizing adverse effects.
Trial medication at optimum dose for 4-6 weeks before considering it ineffective. Always check for treatment adherence.
Patients on long-term antipsychotic treatment should be monitored regularly for symptom relief, functioning, and any adverse effects.
Ensure patient safety and manage concurrent medical conditions.
Provide psycho-education. Patients and families need to understand the phases of mood disturbance and how to self-monitor for extreme mood states.
Encourage a regular sleep cycle and encourage patient to avoid alcohol and psychoactive substances.

For acute mania, if the patient is on an antidepressant, it should quickly be tapered and discontinued, balancing the risks of withdrawal syndrome against the impact of the antidepressant worsening the mania.
Lithium or sodium valproate should be considered in individuals with acute mania for mood stabilization.
In women planning a pregnancy, pregnant or breastfeeding, lithium and sodium valproate should be avoided. Low dose haloperidol should be considered with caution.
For acute depression in bipolar disorder, the treatment of choice is a mood stabilizer agent (sodium valproate, lithium).
Once the patient is adequately treated with a mood stabilizer, an antidepressant agent can be added to help further alleviate depressive symptoms.
Maintenance treatment with a mood stabilizer should continue for at least 2 years after the last episode of bipolar disorder.

medical_records.primary_topic: Sad or Low Mood including Depression
clinical_signs: Fatigue or loss of energy or tiredness
Guilt or loss of self confidence
Suicidal thoughts or acts or thoughts of death
Feeling tense, anxious, excessively worried, or frightened
Unexplained somatic symptoms (tingling, numbness, shortness of breath, gastrointestinal distress, palpitations, aches and pains)
suspected_conditions: Bipolar disorder - depressive episode or depressive phase
Depressive symptoms due to general medical condition

step_by_step_guide: Assess for suicide risk and ensure safety.
Provide counselling. Counselling and psychotherapy often are sufficient for the treatment of a depressive episode of mild intensity.
Initiate management of any co-morbid conditions (e.g. treat substance use disorders, medical conditions, and pain).
Fluoxetine or tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) should be considered as treatment in individuals with moderate to severe depressive disorder.
In adolescents with depressive episodes, do not consider antidepressant medication as first-line treatment. Psychosocial treatments, including psychotherapy, are preferable.
When starting antidepressants, monitor patient for risk of harm to self or others at every contact.
Dosage adjustment of medication should be based on symptom response and tolerability.
If the patient has a poor symptom response by week 12 despite good adherence to the maximum tolerated dose of a medication, switch to another antidepressant.
In adult individuals with depressive disorders who have benefited from initial antidepressant treatment, do not consider ending the antidepressant treatment before 9-12 months after recovery.

clinical_signs: Feeling tense, anxious, apprehensive or frightened

step_by_step_guide: Address underlying medical conditions that could cause the anxiety symptoms, including possible modification of prescribed medication.
Discuss health behaviours that may reduce anxiety, including eliminating caffeine, regulating sleep hygiene, and decreasing the use of alcohol and substances.

Benzodiazepines can be used for the short-term treatment of an acute episode of severe anxiety caused by a stressful life event. Longer-term treatment (beyond 2 weeks) with benzodiazepines is not indicated.
SSRI medications are useful for the longer-term treatment of most anxiety disorders.
Amitriptyline and clomipramine can be useful for selected anxiety disorders.
In non-specialized care, relaxation training should be considered as treatment for anxiety symptoms of at least 2 weeks duration.
Cognitive behaviour therapy is an excellent treatment for anxiety disorders.

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 20 mg of fluoxetine daily. May start at 10 mg daily to reduce the risk of side-effects that undermine adherence, and then increase to 20 mg as tolerated. Dose may be gradually increased up to 60 mg as necessary and tolerable.
side_effects_or_warnings: Increased risk for agitation and suicidal ideation and behaviour
Precipitation of mania in patients with a history of mania or bipolar disorder

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 50 mg of amitriptyline at bedtime. Increase by 25-50 mg every 1-2 weeks, aiming for 100-150 mg by 4-6 weeks. Maximum dose of 200 mg given in divided doses or a single dose at night.
side_effects_or_warnings: Increased risk for agitation and suicidal ideation and behaviour
Precipitation of mania in patients with a history of mania or bipolar disorder
Do not give if there is a history of arrhythmia or recent heart attack

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 1.5-3 mg once daily. Depending on symptom severity, symptom response, and tolerability, can be titrated up to 20 mg daily. Typical effective dose: 3-20 mg daily. Use lowest effective dose.
side_effects_or_warnings: Acute dystonic reaction or severe muscle spasm
Involuntary muscular movements that may not be reversible (tardive dyskinesia)
Increased risk of death in elderly patients with dementia

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 75 mg at night. Depending on symptom severity, symptom response, and tolerability, can be titrated up to 300 mg daily. Typical effective dose is 75-300 mg daily, but up to 1000 mg may be necessary in severe cases.
side_effects_or_warnings: Severe hypotension resulting in falls (especially in elderly or medically ill patients)
Acute dystonic reaction or severe muscle spasm
Involuntary muscular movements that may not be reversible (tardive dyskinesia)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Start with 12.5 mg deep intramuscular injection in the gluteal region. Typical effective dose is 12.5-100 mg IM every 2 to 5 weeks.
side_effects_or_warnings: Neuromuscular side-effects can take a long time to clear after drug discontinuation
Avoid for acute treatment because it is difficult to titrate the dose in accordance with symptom response and tolerability

medical_records.primary_topic: Diazepam (for short-term treatment only for acute anxiety)
medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate treatment with 2 mg of diazepam 1 to 3 times daily. May increase gradually if needed to 5-10 mg twice daily. Maximum dose is 20 mg during a 24-hour period.
side_effects_or_warnings: Should not be given to patients using alcohol

medical_records.adult_dosing: Initiate a dose of 10-25 mg daily at bedtime. Increase in divided doses by 25 mg every 4-7 days to a therapeutic dose of 150-200 mg in divided doses or a single dose at night.
side_effects_or_warnings: Side-effects, contraindications, and cautions similar to amitriptyline

medical_records.primary_topic: Home Care and Psycho-education for Dementia
home_care_instructions: Ensure the patient's safety with appropriate environment and level of supervision.
Ensure that the patient is taking medications as prescribed.
Protect wandering patients, and supervise any cigarette smoking that occurs.
Use strategies to prevent falls (e.g. reduce clutter on floors, re-evaluate medications that lead to orthostatic hypotension, encourage tailored physical activity to maintain mobility).
Reorient the patient and encourage the presence of familiar objects and people.
Emphasize routines and provide lighting that corresponds with day and night.
Provide a clock and calendar in the room to help keep patients oriented to time and to the day of the week.
Ensure that individuals who require eyeglasses or hearing aids wear these to help lessen confusion and disorientation.
return_to_clinic_warning_signs: Behavioural symptoms persist or there is a clear and imminent risk of harm to the patient with severe and distressing symptoms.

clinical_signs: Multiple physical complaints that cannot be explained by either a known medical disorder after appropriate clinical investigations or by another mental disorder
Pupils not equally reactive to light
Blood or fluid from ear or nose
immediate_actions: Protect from fall or injury.
Manage airway and assist into recovery position.
Give oxygen 5 litres.
Call for help but do not leave patient alone.
Give glucose (if blood glucose is low or unknown).
Check (then monitor and record) level of consciousness on AVPU scale.
If convulsing: Give diazepam IV or rectally.
Do not move neck if cervical spine injury possible - immobilize spine.
Give oxygen 5 litres.
Log-roll patient when moving.
Expose patient fully.
Look/feel for deformity of skull.
Call for help from district clinician/surgeon.
Treat tension pneumothorax with emergency needle decompression if present.

medical_records.primary_topic: Unconscious or decreased level of consciousness
suspected_conditions: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) or hyperosmolar non-ketotic (HONK) coma

clinical_signs: Usually occurs at term, during delivery, or immediately following delivery
History of hazardous alcohol use or use of sedative-hypnotic drugs

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
CSF pressure manometer or IV tubing and pole
step_by_step_guide: Position the patient lying on one side with the spine flexed (draw shoulders forward and bring thighs towards the abdomen).
Clean area with antiseptic.
Anaesthetize the skin and subcutaneous tissues with lidocaine.
Gently introduce the spinal needle with bevel turned upward and angled slightly towards the head. Slowly advance.
When the subarachnoid space is entered, there may be a slight give. At this point, the stylet should be carefully withdrawn to confirm the flow of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).
Measure opening pressure (usually between 10-20 cm H20).
Collect 2 ml CSF in each of 4 collection tubes.
Replace stylet and remove the needle. Apply pressure with sterile dressing for a few minutes.
contraindications: Increased intracranial pressure evidenced by focal neurological signs, papilloedema, altered mental status, or recent seizure.

equipment_needed: locally available material (towel rolls, newspaper, sandbags, or bags of IV fluids)
step_by_step_guide: apply cervical collar or stabilize the neck with locally available material.
keep the patient lying on a flat surface.
prevent the neck from moving with locally available materials or cervical collar if available.
if patient vomits, turn whole patient on their side, keeping head in line with the body.
keep someone with patient at all times to watch the airway.
if need to move patient use log roll technique.
side_effects_or_warnings: Aspiration if patient vomits and is not log-rolled onto their side.

step_by_step_guide: Place bed sheet under the pelvis.
Pull over the great trochanters/iliac wings - cross over anteriorly.
Pull tight and tie.

medical_records.adult_dosing: Convulsions: 10 mg IV slowly, or rectally if no IV access; can be repeated after 10 minutes if convulsion does not stop. DO NOT give IM. Acute alcohol withdrawal: Up to 20 mg, according to the severity of alcohol withdrawal, every 1-2 hours until patient is calm and mildly sedated.
side_effects_or_warnings: Sedation and other side-effects that appear similar to alcohol intoxication
respiratory depression (usually due to an excessive dose)
drowsiness and light-headedness the next day

medical_records.adult_dosing: Oral: Start at 150-200 mg daily, increase by increments of 25-30 mg to reach maintenance at 200-400 mg daily. Loading dose: IV (slowly over 60 min): 15 mg/kg in normal saline, at a rate of not more than 50 mg/minute.
contraindications: Avoid parenteral use in sinus bradycardia, sinoatrial block, second- and third-degree heart block, Adams-Stokes syndrome.
side_effects_or_warnings: insomnia, transient nervousness, tremor, paraesthesia, dizziness, headache
severe allergic reaction (including toxic epidermal necrolysis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Status epilepticus Loading dose: IV: 5-15 mg/kg bolus over 1 hour. Epilepsy Oral: Initiate at 60 mg daily; then maintain at 60-180 mg daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: With IV: hypotension, respiratory depression, laryngospasm

medical_records.adult_dosing: Generalized tonic-clonic seizures, partial seizures Oral: initially 100-200 mg 1-2 times daily (tablets) OR 50-100 mg every 6 hours as oral suspension THEN Increase gradually by ~200 mg every week as needed (usual maintenance dose is 400-1400 mg daily in divided doses).
contraindications: history of sensitivity to tricyclic antidepressants, oxcarbazepine, or other structurally related drugs
use of MAO-Is within past 2 weeks

medical_records.adult_dosing: Psychoses (including schizophrenia): Oral: Initially 1.5-3 mg once daily (typical effective dose is 3-20 mg daily). Short-term adjunctive management of agitation, violent behaviour, severe anxiety: IM/oral: 2 mg every hour up to 5 doses (maximum dose 10 mg).
side_effects_or_warnings: Extrapyramidal side-effects: Acute dystonic reaction or severe muscle spasm, stiffness, tremor, motor restlessness, agitation, Parkinson's syndrome

medical_records.adult_dosing: IV: 100 mcg in a single dose OR IM: 400 mcg in a single dose OR SC: 800 mcg in a single dose. May repeat every 5 minutes up to 3 times (maximum 10 mg). If response (i.e. respiratory rate >10/minute), start IV infusion: 0.4 mg/hour for 12 hours.

medical_records.primary_topic: WHO Analgesic Ladder for Chronic Pain
step_by_step_guide: Give analgesics by mouth if possible, as it is the easiest route.
Give pain killers at fixed time intervals (by the clock) to prevent pain from returning; do not give analgesics for chronic pain on a PRN basis.
For breakthrough pain, give an extra rescue dose in addition to the regular schedule.

Follow the 3-step analgesic ladder: Step 1 (Non-opioid like paracetamol or ibuprofen), Step 2 (Opioid for mild/moderate pain like codeine plus non-opioid), Step 3 (Opioid for moderate/severe pain like oral morphine plus non-opioid).
If pain is not controlled by a step 2 analgesic, do not change to another stage 2 analgesic but instead move up to a step 3 analgesic.
Do not use Step 2 and Step 3 opioids at the same time.
Reduce or cease chronic opioid treatment gradually (not more than 10% per week) if the underlying condition improves, to avoid withdrawal symptoms.

medical_records.adult_dosing: 30-60 mg every 4 hours. Maximum daily dose for pain 240 mg. Consider switch to morphine when a dose of 180 mg is reached.
side_effects_or_warnings: Unless diarrhoea is present, always give a laxative to avoid constipation.

medical_records.adult_dosing: 2-4 mg per day for all situations apart from raised intracranial pressure. For raised intracranial pressure, start at 24 mg daily and reduce by 2 mg daily to lowest effective maintenance dose.

medical_records.primary_topic: Morphine IV Infusion for Refractory Severe Acute Pain
step_by_step_guide: Administer a bolus dose of 2 mg initially.
Follow with a continuous IV infusion at 1 mg/hour using a strictly controlled rate (e.g., metal gate-clamp or closely monitored pump).
Titrate the infusion hourly as needed for pain control, usually between 5-35 mg/hour.
Monitor responsiveness, airway, respiratory rate, BP, and pulse every 15 minutes.
Slow the infusion if the patient begins to show signs of somnolence.
Stop the infusion immediately if there is respiratory depression (RR <16) or hypotension (SBP <90).
If systolic BP <100, SpO2 <90, or respiratory rate <10/minute, determine if patient needs more fluids and administer naloxone.

medical_records.primary_topic: Palliative Management of Cough and Dyspnoea
step_by_step_guide: Control bronchospasm by giving bronchodilators via metered-dose inhaler with spacer. Stop bronchodilators in terminal care if the patient cannot use them or has very shallow/laboured breathing.
Relieve excessive sputum with steam inhalations; if sputum is >30 ml/day, use forced expiratory technique (huffing) with postural drainage.
For a bothersome dry cough, give codeine tablets 5-10 mg 4 times daily.
For terminal dyspnoea not responding to codeine, give oral morphine 2.5-5 mg.
Titrate the dose of morphine to its effect in relieving dyspnoea. For a patient not on morphine for pain, start with 2.5 mg 4-6 hourly.
If there is heart failure or excess fluid with pitting oedema, give furosemide 40-80 mg orally.
For anxiety or terminal agitation, consider giving small doses of diazepam 2.5-5 mg.
Assess the patient for possible causes of hiccups, such as extensive oro-esophageal candidiasis, ascites, organomegaly, or metabolic disorders.
Prescribe metoclopramide 10 mg every 8 hours in combination with an antacid and antiflatulant (e.g. aluminium hydroxide).
If hiccups do not respond, administer haloperidol 2.5-5 mg.
If the patient has ascites, carry out therapeutic abdominal paracentesis.

medical_records.primary_topic: Preventive Home Care for Bedridden Patients
home_care_instructions: Use a soft toothbrush to gently brush the patient's teeth, tongue, palate, and gums to remove debris.
Rinse the mouth with diluted salt water after eating and at bedtime (usually 3-4 times daily).
Use petroleum jelly to moisten the lips when dry.
Change the patient's position on the bed often, ideally every 1 or 2 hours, and use pillows or cushions to maintain the position.
Keep the bedding clean and dry, and look for damaged skin (change of colour) on the back, shoulders, and hips every day.
Perform simple range-of-motion exercises on limbs and joints on both sides at least twice daily.
Protect the joint by holding the limb above and below it, and bend, straighten, and move joints slowly without causing pain.

step_by_step_guide: Respect the wishes of the patient and family regarding whether the patient wants to die at home or in the hospital.
Offer hospice-like care and continued support from the clinical team to families who want to keep the patient at home.
Manage pain and other symptoms, including cough or difficult breathing, using morphine to provide significant relief from respiratory insufficiency.
Provide nutritional support with small and frequent meals. Do not force feeding tubes if they would not add to the overall quality of the patient's life.
Treat fever only if the patient is uncomfortable, as fever treatment is not always necessary at the end of life.
Continue preventive measures such as oral care, prevention of bedsores, bathing, and scheduled movement of the bedridden patient.
Provide bereavement care and support to the family, including practical help with planning for the care of their children and funeral rites.

immediate_actions: Manage airway
Give oxygen 5 litres
If inadequate breathing, assist ventilation with bag valve mask
Help patient assume position of comfort
If wheezing, give salbutamol
If foreign body aspiration, treat choking patient
If suspect anaphylaxis, give 1:1000 epinephrine (adrenaline) IM

clinical_signs: Capillary refill longer than three seconds
SBP <90 mmHg or pulse >110 per minute
immediate_actions: Give oxygen 5 litres if respiratory distress or SpO2 <90
Insert IV, give 1 litre bolus crystalloid (LR or NS) then reassess
Keep warm (cover)
If in second half pregnancy, place on her side (preferably on the left), not on back
If anaphylaxis, give 1:1000 epinephrine (adrenaline) IM

clinical_signs: Chest pain with exertion (crushing in nature, radiating to jaw or arm)
Associated with nausea and shortness of breath
Crushing chest pain (pressure, tightness) radiating to the jaw or arm at rest
Crackles on auscultation, bronchial breath sounds
Tearing pain radiating to the back, abdomen or between shoulder blades
suspected_conditions: Acute coronary syndrome (unstable angina, non-ST elevation or ST elevation myocardial infarction)

clinical_signs: Episodic chest tightness, shortness of breath, and diffuse wheezing
Night-time symptoms and cough are common
Increase in baseline breathlessness, cough, sputum quantity or purulence
Atypical presentation with diffuse wheezing and crackles (rales)
Fluid overload (elevated JVP, lower extremity oedema)

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
lidocaine with epinephrine (5–10 ml syringe, 23- to 25-gauge needle)
chest tube and underwater seal drainage system (or one-way valve device and drainage bag)
suture material (0 or 1–0 sutures required to anchor tube)
needle driver, large curved artery forceps
step_by_step_guide: Position the patient lying face up with arm of the involved side raised over the head. If the patient is unable to lie down due to respiratory distress, he or she may sit up in a bed or chair.
Choose the site, usually the 5th or 6th intercostal space at the midaxillary line.
Prepare the skin with antiseptic.
Using lidocaine, infiltrate the skin and muscle. Note the length of needle needed to enter the pleural cavity.
Aspirate fluid from the chest cavity to confirm position of the needle.
Make a 3–4 cm horizontal incision just above the rib to avoid damaging the vessels under the lower part of the rib.
Use more lidocaine to anaesthetize the intercostal tissues and pleura at the site of insertion.
Use blunt dissection to penetrate the intercostal tissue to the pleura. Insert the closed clamp over the top of the rib and, once past the rib, open and spread to dissect.
Insert a finger into the tunnel to confirm that it has penetrated through to the pleural space.
Use the same forceps to grasp the tube at its tip and introduce it into the chest. Never use a sharp instrument to introduce the tube.
Close the incision with interrupted skin sutures. Use 1 stitch to anchor the tube.
Connect the tube to the underwater seal drainage system and mark the initial level of fluid in the drainage bottle.

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
lidocaine (5–10 ml syringe, 23- to 25-gauge needle, 20-gauge needle)
16-gauge needle; obese patients may require longer needle – consider using a spinal needle
30 ml syringe – may need larger (50–100 ml) for large effusions
step_by_step_guide: The patient should be seated with arms and head supported (e.g. sitting backwards on a chair).
Localize the pleural effusion by determining the level where dullness to percussion begins when percussing the posterior chest from top to bottom.
Choose a site on the posterior chest in the mid-scapular line (approximately 5–10 cm lateral to the spine).
Clean the area with antiseptic; be sure to maintain strict aseptic technique.
The skin and subcutaneous tissue should be anaesthetized with lidocaine using a 25-gauge needle.
Using a longer, 20-gauge needle, anaesthetize the pleura, and gently aspirate until pleural fluid is noted in the syringe.
In the previous puncture site, insert a 16-gauge needle attached to a large syringe or to a drip giving set.
Advance the needle slowly, keeping it above the top of the rib. Aspirate gently while advancing the needle.
When pleural fluid is noted, place a haemostat on the needle to prevent it from accidentally advancing forward.
Remove the necessary amount of pleural fluid (usually 100 ml for diagnostic studies). Do not remove more than 1500 ml of fluid at once.
Gently remove the needle.
contraindications: pre-existing infection at the site of needle insertion

equipment_needed: sterile gloves and sterile towels or drapes
step_by_step_guide: If possible, this procedure should be done by an experienced operator with guidance from fluoroscopy or echocardiography or ultrasound.
After the area has been sterilized and anaesthetized, the needle should be inserted 1 cm to the left of the xiphoid process, and directed towards the left shoulder.
Maintain a 30° angle to the skin to avoid the pleura and nearby arteries.
While the needle is being inserted, aspiration should be gently and intermittently attempted until fluid is withdrawn.
No more than 1 litre of fluid at a time should be aspirated in order to avoid acute right ventricular dilatation.
contraindications: traumatic haemopericardium, haemopericardium due to aortic dissection, and purulent pericarditis (surgical approach preferred)
side_effects_or_warnings: Myocardial or coronary vessel laceration may present in a delayed fashion as hemopericardium or cardiac tamponade
Acute left or right ventricular failure with pulmonary oedema
Puncture of peritoneal cavity or abdominal organs

medical_records.adult_dosing: By nebulizer: 10–20 mg OR IV: 0.5 mg (500 mcg). Administration should be slow, over 15–20 minutes. If neither of these are available, give salbutamol 1200 mcg by metered-dose inhaler with spacer (12 puffs).
side_effects_or_warnings: hyperglycaemia and hypokalaemia after high doses

medical_records.adult_dosing: IM (1:1000): 0.5 ml in a single dose if 50 kg (0.3 ml if 30 kg, 0.4 ml if 40 kg) THEN may repeat at 5-minute intervals. IV infusion: See vasopressor dosing table. IM: 0.5 mg (0.5 ml of 1:1000)
side_effects_or_warnings: tissue necrosis (if injected IM or SC)

medical_records.adult_dosing: Infusion, intravenous: 200 mg in 5 ml ampoule. IV: See vasopressor dosing table and instructions in Section 3.1.

Oral: Initially 20–80 mg daily in morning. Maintenance dose: 20–40 mg daily (may be increased to 80 mg daily or more in resistant oedema). IV (slow): 20–50 mg (if necessary, increase by 20 mg steps every 2 hours).
contraindications: precomatose states associated with liver cirrhosis
side_effects_or_warnings: deafness (with rapid administration of large parenteral doses and in renal impairment)

medical_records.primary_topic: Abnormal Vaginal Bleeding or Pelvic Pain (Positive Pregnancy Test)
clinical_signs: Missed period and abnormal bleeding or spotting
Unilateral or generalized abdominal pain with rebound tenderness
Uterus normal size or slightly enlarged, cervix closed
History of recent spontaneous or induced termination of pregnancy or recent delivery
Uterine tenderness, possible foul odour, or purulent discharge
Abdominal distension and abnormal uterine contour

step_by_step_guide: Stabilize the patient prior to the procedure. Start IV antibiotics prior to procedure.
Treat with benzyl penicillin 5 million units IV every 6 hours OR ampicillin 2-3 g IV every 6 hours, combined with gentamicin 4-6 mg/kg IV daily and clindamycin 900 mg IV every 8 hours.
If clindamycin is not available, use metronidazole 500 mg IV every 8 hours. If gentamicin is contraindicated, use ceftriaxone 1 gram IV daily.
Perform prompt evacuation of the uterus. Vacuum aspiration is the recommended technique for surgical abortion up to 12-14 weeks.
After 12-14 weeks, refer to an experienced provider for dilatation and evacuation. If not available, use misoprostol (400 mcg sublingually or 600 mcg orally).
Change to oral antibiotics for discharge: doxycycline 100 mg orally every 12 hours plus amoxicillin-clavulanic acid 500 + 125 mg orally every 12 hours for a total of 14 days of treatment.
Review outpatient cases after 48 hours of treatment and hospitalize if fever and pain persist.

medical_records.primary_topic: Abnormal Vaginal Bleeding or Pelvic Pain (Negative Pregnancy Test)
clinical_signs: Unilateral tenderness, possible palpable adnexal mass
Sudden onset pain often preceded by strenuous activity or sex
Clinical presentation similar to bacterial PID but with past history of or contact with TB
Ascites with abdominal distension and fluid thrill
Frequent history of abnormal foul-smelling discharge
Adnexal mass (tender, cystic, solid, or mixed)
Missed menstrual period and abnormal bleeding
Heavy or prolonged menses, urinary frequency
Loss of weight, presence of ascites
Mass with solid consistency, irregular, and fixed (suggests cancer)

step_by_step_guide: Maintain a high index of suspicion in women with lower abdominal pain and uterine, adnexal, or cervical motion tenderness.
Perform a pregnancy test to rule out ectopic pregnancy.
Administer Ceftriaxone 250 mg IM single dose (or Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally single dose, if sensitivities support it).
Add Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days (or Tetracycline).
Add Metronidazole 400-500 mg orally twice daily for 14 days.
Advise the patient and her partner(s) to abstain from sexual intercourse until the treatment has been completed.
Offer counseling and testing for HIV, and test for syphilis.
Evaluate and treat partner(s) presumptively for gonorrhoea and Chlamydia.
Follow up outpatients no later than 72 hours (24 hours if fever is present) and admit to the hospital if the condition fails to improve.
Wash the cervix with a diluted solution (3-5%) of acetic acid.
View the cervix with the naked eye to look for abnormalities (pre-cancerous or cancerous lesions turn white).
If VIA is positive, assess eligibility for cryotherapy: lesion must be covered by cryoprobe with no more than 2 mm beyond its edges, fully visible with no extension into endocervix or vagina.
Exclude cryotherapy if there is suspicion of invasive cancer, glandular dysplasia, pregnancy, untreated PID, or menses.
Perform cryotherapy (takes approximately 15 minutes for double-freeze method).
Advise the patient that abstinence is advisable for 4 weeks after the procedure until complete healing has occurred. Provide condoms if abstinence is not possible.
Schedule follow-up within 6 weeks to ensure good healing.
If VIA is positive and the lesion is not suitable for cryotherapy, refer for colposcopy or LEEP/conization.

medical_records.primary_topic: PMTCT: Maternal AZT + Infant ARV Prophylaxis (Option A)
step_by_step_guide: Assess mother for ART eligibility. If not eligible for lifelong ART, start maternal AZT 300 mg twice daily from as early as 14 weeks of gestation.
At onset of labour, provide single-dose NVP (sd-NVP) 200 mg + AZT 300 mg twice daily + 3TC 150 mg twice daily.
Continue maternal AZT 300 mg twice daily + 3TC 150 mg twice daily for 7 days postpartum.
(If maternal AZT was provided for more than 4 weeks during pregnancy, omission of the sd-NVP and AZT+3TC tail can be considered; continue maternal AZT during labour until delivery).
For breastfeeding infants: Give daily NVP from birth (within 6-12 hours) until 1 week after all exposure to breast milk has ended.
For infants receiving replacement feeding only: Give daily NVP or sd-NVP + twice daily AZT from birth until 4-6 weeks of age.

medical_records.primary_topic: PMTCT: Maternal Triple ARV Prophylaxis (Option B)
step_by_step_guide: Assess mother for ART eligibility. If not eligible for lifelong ART, initiate maternal triple ARV prophylaxis from as early as 14 weeks gestation.
Select an appropriate triple ARV regimen (e.g., AZT+3TC+LPV/r, AZT+3TC+ABC, AZT+3TC+EFV, or TDF+3TC+EFV). Avoid EFV during the first trimester.
Continue the maternal triple ARV prophylaxis until delivery, or if breastfeeding, continue until 1 week after all infant exposure to breast milk has ended.
For all infants (breastfeeding or replacement feeding): Give daily NVP or twice daily AZT from birth until 4-6 weeks of age.

medical_records.pediatric_dosing: Birth to 6 weeks: Birth weight 2000-2500 g: 10 mg (1 ml) once daily. Birth weight >2500 g: 15 mg (1.5 ml) once daily. >6 weeks to 6 months: 20 mg (2 ml) once daily. >6 months to 9 months: 30 mg (3 ml) once daily. >9 months to 1 week after end of breastfeeding: 40 mg (4 ml) once daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: Low birth weight infants should receive NVP 2 mg/kg once daily as a starting dose.
A bottle of liquid should be used within 6 months of opening.

medical_records.pediatric_dosing: Birth to 6 weeks: Birth weight 2000-2500 g: 10 mg (1 ml) twice daily. Birth weight >2500 g: 15 mg (1.5 ml) twice daily.
side_effects_or_warnings: Low birth weight infants should receive mg/kg dosing.
Stable at room temperature, but needs storage in a glass jar and is light sensitive.

home_care_instructions: Eat smaller portions of food more frequently.
Drink lots of fluids.
Eat bland and low fat food, as fatty foods may delay gastric emptying.
Serve cold food if the smell of hot food is noxious.
Use ginger drinks and pyridoxine (vitamin B6) to help reduce nausea.
Continue taking ARV drugs, and repeat the dose if you vomit whole tablets.

clinical_signs: Leakage associated with cough, sneeze, laugh
Bladder never feels empty, post-void dribbling
Frequent urination with large amounts of urine (polyuria)
